notes.ref: Order
notes.title: Order
notes.body:
# [Order](https://github.com/geetduggal/order) A specialized, opinionated, local-first notes app on top of an Obsidian-compatible vault: masonry of cards, in-place WYSIWYG editing, drill-down filtering by Areas and Notable Folders, and list folders that render bullet wikilinks as a drag-reorderable card grid. ## Desktop and iOS MVP 🍾 * [x] MVP of Desktop UX * [x] Publishing Support * [x] Newspaper style layout * [x] Redirect personal home * [x] Create transition scaffolding for personal vault * [x] Side bar should be collapsible on mobile * [x] iOS MVP * [x] Wikilink support * [x] Permalink support ## UX * [x] Images are a mess: kumake them more Obsidian friendly and beautiful * [x] Fun themes * [x] Day view * [ ] AI Skills for Calendar import (and export) * [ ] First class published link capabilities (calendar events, doc links in Notion, Google Docs, Medium, etc) * [x] Fix numbers in the WP 5.1 theme * [x] Home button resurrection * [x] Floating folder selection issue * [ ] Folder maintenance * [ ] Add wiki links easily to first class lists * [ ] Don't show metadata on full screen mode * [x] Calendar drag-and-drop improves - [x] Recent folders - [ ] Unify icon and other behaviors - [x] Notable folder picker - [ ] Full text search &| Note Title Search - [ ] Maybe clear filters should be available in dock or something - [x] Create, delete, order: areas, categories, notable folders - [ ] Convert to list - [ ] When creating an event, filters must be removed so event is visible or the event is created with the filter - [ ] iOS publishing - [ ] Duplicate events - [ ] iOS shortcut - [x] New note ease - [ ] Date selection in stream view - [x] Mobile calendar view buttons - [x] Order list item newline issue - [x] iOS vertical spacing issues - [x] iOS grid drag and drop - [ ] Open folder in Finder/Files/Terminal - [x] Sort/order filter pills - [x] Circle icons are on everything - [x] YAML frontmatter view - [x] Highlight unpublished - [x] Task-list spacing issue - [x] More prominent clear all filter - [x] Resolve vertical spacing of list items - [x] Support YouTube embeds - [x] Filter support also works on sidebar - [x] ~~Sidebar is resizable~~ - [x] Finish Readwise and Highlights bases - [x] Content and asset management philosophy/approach - [x] Floating close etc on iOS full screen - [x] Dark mode - [x] `Jump to note lower` - [x] ~~Pinned notes vs Notable folder~~ - [x] Mobile top of mind - [x] Weekly / daily view as default on Desktop / Mobile - [x] Scale-up handling of files, caching, etc - [x] Changes to files externally should show up in the UI immediately - [x] Pill selection should only be for notable folders - [x] Generally more prominent buttons, icons - [ ] iOS Widget ## Road Show * [ ] Deep pass-through of documentation. In particular, I don't want this to be seen as a competitor to Obsidian, more of a compliment to apps like it in An opinionated, powerful, and modern local-first compatible tool * [ ] Prepare packages, logos, and website for a cleaner Beta release
[Repo on GitHub](https://github.com/geetduggal/order)

notes.ref: Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting
notes.title: Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- [Profit First](https://mikemichalowicz.com/profit-first/) is a method of accounting for small businesses that prioritizes business health by enforcing prioritization of core business expenses such as taxes, owner compensation, and profit. [[2024-01-07]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkkxrpzhcb5kgf38kh506z36))

frontmatter.author: Serge Wroclawski
frontmatter.title: Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting
frontmatter.summary: Plain Text Accounting is a method of bookkeeping that stores account and transaction information in plain text files. Profit First is an accounting method that prioritizes core business expenses and recommends using different bank accounts to allocate income. Integrating Plain Text Accounting with Profit First is straightforward if you have multiple bank accounts, but if you have a single account, it becomes more challenging. One approach is to use "virtual postings" in Plain Text Accounting software to create virtual accounts that mirror the Profit First accounts. However, this method requires recording transactions twice and can be more difficult to verify and balance. Another option is to find a bank that supports multiple accounts and use the Profit First model directly.

Most of these are just random thoughts and ideas that get put onto the scratch pad. Eventually it will get into my planning book and then it will become a digital output. But the digital for me now has very much is the output side, whereas the planning side is the paper side.

notes.ref: Mount Diablo - Wikipedia
notes.title: Mount Diablo - Wikipedia
notes.body:
## Highlights
- Mount Diablo is a mountain of the Diablo Range, in Contra Costa County of the eastern San Francisco Bay Area in Northern California. It is south of Clayton and northeast of Danville. It is an isolated upthrust peak of 3,849 feet (1,173 meters), visible from most of the San Francisco Bay Area. Mount Diablo appears from many angles to be a double pyramid and has many subsidiary peaks. The largest and closest is North Peak, the other half of the double pyramid, which is nearly as high in elevation at 3,557 feet (1,084 m), and is about one mile (1.6 kilometers) northeast of the main summit. [[2022-07-02]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518668752/19957614))
- Countless peaks in the state are taller, but Mount Diablo has a remarkable visual prominence for a mountain of such low elevation. Its looming presence over much of the Bay Area, delta, and Central Valley, and good visibility even from the Mother Lode, all key regions during the gold rush and early statehood, made it an important landmark for mapping and navigation. The summit is used as the reference datum for land surveying in much of northern California and Nevada. [[2022-07-02]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518668752/19957618))
- Mount Diablo is sacred to many California Native American peoples. According to Miwok mythology and Ohlone mythology, it was the point of creation [[2022-07-02]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518668752/19957621))

frontmatter.author: San Mateo and Bay
frontmatter.title: Mount Diablo - Wikipedia

notes.ref: How to Plan Better Simple Analog System Cal Newport-2
notes.title: How to Plan Better | Simple Analog System | Cal Newport
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/How to Plan Better Simple Analog System Cal Newport-2.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- but I feel like there are three big ones. The first one is a calendar that's completely functional and shows everything. And in my book, I refer to that as like one master calendar. And that can be it sounds so straightforward, like of course I have a calendar, but a lot of people are actually consulting multiple places, even on a day-to-day level, to actually figure out where they're supposed to be. So, master calendar is number one. Number two is a really robust task management system. I've coined the term
  airtight task management because I want to communicate that like you know exactly what's coming in, where to look for it, how often to look for it, and where to put it so that you know you will see it. And to me, that's the part where you're sort of preventing the moth eating like things coming at you from really um getting too much of your time and attention and you know putting the tasks in their place where they belong. And then finally, you need a fantastic and robust goal setting system. Um, you talk about yours in like a multi-level
  scale planning, and I have a very similar version of that called nested goals. It has a couple more levels than yours [[2026-04-18]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kph8h9q99wen50kqdeahx9wp))

frontmatter.author: Cal Newport
frontmatter.title: How to Plan Better | Simple Analog System | Cal Newport
frontmatter.summary: Planning helps manage tasks and reduce stress by organizing work in a clear way. Using a calendar and daily lists makes it easier to focus on what matters each day. Breaking goals into smaller tasks and reviewing them regularly keeps progress steady and manageable.

notes.ref: The 10% of AI Tools That Drive 90% of My Results
notes.title: The 10% of AI Tools That Drive 90% of My Results
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/The 10% of AI Tools That Drive 90% of My Results.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Chachib doesn't decide which instructions matter. It just follows them. Here's a second simpler example. Sometimes when you explicitly tell Gemini to search the web, it just doesn't, which is wild since Gemini and Google search are both Google products, right? Whereas with ChachiT, when you enable web search, it performs the web search every single time. I know this is
  a small example, but it's downstream from Chachib's core superpower. Obedience means you can trust the behavior you ask for. [[2026-01-20]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kffnycc58scjzgg59ksgstxc))

frontmatter.author: Jeff Su
frontmatter.title: The 10% of AI Tools That Drive 90% of My Results
frontmatter.summary: The author uses about 10 AI tools, each best for a specific task, to get most of his work done efficiently. Everyday AI tools like Chachi, Gemini, and Claude excel in handling complex instructions, mixed media, and coding respectively. Specialist AI tools like Perplexity and Notebook LM focus on fast, accurate information retrieval and checking facts against sources.

notes.ref: Three Useful Alternatives to the Eisenhower Matrix
notes.title: Three Useful Alternatives to the Eisenhower Matrix
notes.body:
## Highlights
- I often have tasks that other people are waiting on — and those should have the priority. Combining “level of impact” with “People waiting?” is sometimes the best way to categorize my tasks [[2024-07-13]] 
- some things are ten times more effective at accomplishing your goal, whatever it is, and some things are only two times as effective [[2024-07-13]] 
- there are much more complex tables than 2x2 that are out there determining many aspects of your existence [[2024-07-13]]

notes.ref: How to Plan Better Simple Analog System Cal Newport
notes.title: How to Plan Better | Simple Analog System | Cal Newport
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/How to Plan Better Simple Analog System Cal Newport.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- So then let's talk about seasonality because this is something in your book best laid plans, the book, not the podcast best plays. Um there's a lot on this and I think we're like very congruent on this idea of moving away from the notion of just year round no variation to your it's just like you're turning the crank at like a certain
  level of intensity and February feels the same as June feels the same as December. >> Um talk to me about varying rhythms, pace, workloads over time. >> Well, first of all, I just love the concept of seasons in general. And I don't know if that's partly because I live in South Florida and I don't really get to experience them, but I just like to really really like think about them and think about how my year makes sense divided up. And I actually kind of talk about different ways that you might think about dividing up your year other than the traditional quarters or even
  trimesters if you're an academic. Um, but I really do like to take a very purposeful like almost half a day kind of planning session four or five times a year. For me, it's five because I like to divide the year up into five pieces and think about what do I want out of the upcoming season and not to assume that, you know, season C is going to be exactly like season A. For me, um, the first season of the year is like January 1st to spring break and that's usually a very go season. And then we kind of have
  a very kid focused season from spring break until the end of the year when we have all that like May stuff and every single kid is in every single competition, whatever. And then summer I treat as much more like let's just be lower key do fun stuff. Um and by the way I didn't mention this previously but I think one other place we differ a little bit is I am very passionate about not just planning my work but planning the fun stuff like planning the gettogethers with friends and the travel and the massage or you know whatever it
  is that I'm trying to build into my life to make it more fun. And so summer might be a time that I have like a lot of fun planned and it's just like a looser time period. Then we have back to school which has that rhythm of like okay kids are going back. We're in our routines. I'm also because I'm in the planning world tend to be really really busy in like January and back to school season. So that kind of makes sense. And then I have what's called reflection season from November 1st to the end of the year where I just feel like the world takes on a different pace. It's a little celebratory. everyone's reflecting and
  um I just like to like acknowledge that as having its own energy. So yes, I'm super super big into a like acknowledging the seasonal flows and b like purposefully setting time to think very hard about what you want each season to be like in advance of that season. >> So wait, so your quintiles are um so you got like new years through spring break, right? Spring break to the end of the school year >> like end of school year. Yeah. Period. Which I agree with you. kind of like I think of it's a time when it's coaching time for me too. It's like I I coach
  multiple different things. Um summer >> summer >> then back to school to like Thanksgiving >> and then until Halloween and then November 1st to December 31st to me just feels a little bit different. >> Well, but you got like holiday Yeah. And you have uh there's like the Thanksgiving holiday. There's going to be like the Christmas holiday. There's going to be and people wind down. What I think a I love it. Um, and I think similarly, and I think what's important here though is because a lot of times when I talk about seasonality, you probably get the same thing. People will
  push back because they'll say, "Well, like my job isn't seasonal." [[2026-03-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kk0hqjgsr3845kx9hxanq5nd))

frontmatter.author: Cal Newport
frontmatter.title: How to Plan Better | Simple Analog System | Cal Newport
frontmatter.summary: Cal Newport explains how using a simple analog system can help plan tasks and goals effectively. He ties tasks to specific times and reviews unfinished tasks daily or weekly. This method reduces stress by focusing only on what needs to be done today or this week.

frontmatter.title: Steve Jobs' Ruthless Secret to Focus — And Why Elon Musk Took It to the Extreme
frontmatter.summary: Steve Jobs focused only on the top three to five tasks he must complete in the next 18 hours. He ignored all distractions, keeping 80% of his time on important work. Elon Musk takes this further by eliminating almost all noise, staying 100% focused every moment he's awake.

notes.ref: What Alcohol Does to Your Body, Brain & Health Huberman Lab Podcast #86
notes.title: What Alcohol Does to Your Body, Brain & Health | Huberman Lab Podcast #86
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/What Alcohol Does to Your Body, Brain & Health Huberman Lab Podcast #86.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Andrew Huberman, and I'm a professor of neurobiology and ophthalmology at Stanford School of Medicine. [[2023-10-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hd52bw7c3w5t1y6dm3cacwv7))
- and how it affects the cells and tissues and organs of your body. Then we'll take a look at some of the epidemiology, that is, how many people are consuming alcohol and how much they're drinking. And then you will be able, I think, to get a good sense of how the alcohol that you're drinking, if you're drinking any at all, is impacting your brain and body and the choices you might want to make about how and when to drink alcohol, or even if you want to eliminate alcohol altogether. Okay, so some basic chemistry and biology of alcohol, and, again, I'll make this very clear even if you don't have a chemistry and biology background. [[2023-10-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hd546r1sqv6xcy3easq6dn6d))
- means is when you drink alcohol, it can pass into all the cells and tissues of your body. It has no trouble just passing right into those cells. So unlike a lot of substances and drugs that actually attach to the surface of cells, to receptors, as they're called, little parking [[2023-10-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hd54a3qn1nbd928jm22e14zb))
- And there's a molecule inside of all of us called NAD, and you may have heard of NAD because it's quite popular, there's a lot of discussion about NAD
  in the longevity literature right now. NAD is present in all our cells from birth until death. The levels of NAD tend to go down across the lifespan. There are ideas that increasing levels of NAD may extend lifespan. A lot of that is still controversial, or, at least we should say, is ongoing in terms of the research. But nonetheless, when you ingest ethanol, NAD and related biochemical pathways are involved in converting that ethanol into something called acetaldehyde, it's broken down into acetaldehyde. And if you thought ethanol was bad, acetaldehyde is particularly bad. Acetaldehyde is poison. It will kill cells. [[2023-10-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hd54cwabkgw9d9mhmpqkzavv))
- at least after the first drink or second drink, there's a slight suppression in the activity of neurons in the prefrontal cortex. This is an area of your neocortex that's involved in thinking and planning and, perhaps above all, in suppression of impulsive behavior. So if you go to a party and they're serving alcohol and people are consuming drinks, what you'll notice is that a few minutes into that party, the volume of people's voices will increase, and that's because people are simply not paying attention
  to their voice modulation, and as other people start speaking more loudly, other people are speaking more loudly. We've all had this experience, right, of going to a party and then you step outside for a moment and you go, "Oh, my goodness, I was shouting." You come home, the next day, you've got a sore throat. It might be that you picked up some sort of bug, some virus or something, but oftentimes it's just the fact that you've been shouting all night just to be heard because as the prefrontal cortex shuts down, people stop modulating their level of speech quite as much. You also notice that people start gesticulating more, people will start standing up and sitting down more, they'll start walking around more,
  if there's music on, people might spontaneously start dancing. All of this is because these areas of the prefrontal cortex normally are providing what's called top-down inhibition. They are releasing a neurotransmitter called GABA onto various parts of the brain that are involved in impulsive motor behavior and thought patterns, and as you shut down the prefrontal cortex, that GABAergic suppression of impulses starts to be released, so people will say things that they want to say without so much [[2023-10-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hd5436a3j06j3zbaex9dvx5p))
- forethought about what they're saying,
  or they might do things that they want to do without really thinking it through quite as much or they might not even remember thinking it through at all, or experience, I should say, thinking it through at all. We haven't talked about blacking out [[2023-10-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hd54t1j2vz4nf6qg3v7yrs59))

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: What Alcohol Does to Your Body, Brain & Health | Huberman Lab Podcast #86
frontmatter.summary: In this episode, I discuss the physiological effects that drinking alcohol has on the brain and body at different levels of consumption and over time. I also describe genetic differences that predispose certain individuals to alcoholism, binge and habit-drinking. I explain alcohol metabolism in simple terms and how it effectively acts as a poison, leading to cellular stress and damage. I then explain that it impacts neuronal function and changes our thinking and behavior – hallmarks of inebriation. I also discuss how alcohol consumption of different amounts impacts inflammation, stress, neurodegeneration, and cancer risk and negatively impacts the gut microbiome, brain thickness, hormone balance, mood and feelings of motivation. Additionally, I discuss the biology of hangovers and describe science-based strategies to mitigate the severity of a hangover. Since alcohol is one of the most widely consumed recreational substances, this episode ought to be of relevance to everyone.  Indeed, even low-to-moderate alcoho...

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: The Science of Creativity & How to Enhance Creative Innovation | Huberman Lab Podcast 103
frontmatter.summary: In this podcast episode, the host discusses the science of creativity and how to enhance creative innovation. The neural circuits responsible for creativity have been defined, and the processes within the brain and body that lead to creativity are well known. Creativity stems from a small subset of neural structures that need to be activated in a specific sequence. The creative process involves both divergent thinking, which is about exploration and generating many ideas, and convergent thinking, which is about focus and persistence to find the correct answer. Dopamine plays a crucial role in both types of thinking, and there are ways to enhance dopamine levels to improve creative thinking. Open monitoring meditation has been shown to enhance divergent thinking, while focus and persistence are key for convergent thinking. Elevating dopamine levels is beneficial for creativity, but too much dopamine can hinder divergent thinking. The podcast also discusses a behavioral tool that can selectively elevate dopamine i...

notes.ref: The Science of Making & Breaking Habits Huberman Lab Podcast #53
notes.title: The Science of Making & Breaking Habits | Huberman Lab Podcast #53
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/The Science of Making & Breaking Habits Huberman Lab Podcast #53.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- And within those scientific literatures, I think there are some real gems that, at least to my knowledge, we haven't paid too much attention to in the popular sphere. So today, we're going to talk about the biology of habit formation and habit breaking. I'm going to boil these down to some very explicit steps that anyone can use. My reasoning for doing that is, first of all, it's the end of 2021. Many people are thinking about new year's resolutions. They're thinking about leaving some things behind from 2021 [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7a0ga8gk2hdv72fctrq4kx))
- What we do habitually makes up much of what we do entirely. In fact, it's estimated that up to 70% of our waking behavior is made up of habitual behavior [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7a28arq7e1bshsnehz56ff))
- so-called identity-based habits where there's a larger, over-arching theme to the habit, where you're trying to become, quote unquote, "a fit person" [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7a3364m5270ytqtjbxqtmh))
- I'm making the distinction is that pretty soon in our discussion today we are going to talk about dopamine, a molecule that's associated with motivation and reward, that we make in our brain, and how different schedules of dopamine release predict where we will stick to a habit or not. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7a41n9eah4t59eyepf18d7))
- Well, in order to form that habit,
  it took some people 18 days and other people 254 days. How did they know when they formed the habit? Well, they were doing it about 85% of the time and they also reported not having to spend that much mental effort in order to get into to the mode of taking a walk after dinner [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7a52e5j0vww7b8qfm61bb0))
- The mystery of why certain people can form certain habits more easily than others? Probably has something to do with how people manage what's called limbic friction [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7a60p8mhbz5qmae370pzmm))
- Limbic friction is a short-hand way that I use to describe the strain that's required
  in order to overcome one of two states within your body. One state is one of anxiousness, where you're really anxious and therefore you can't calm down, you can't relax, and therefore you can't engage in some particular activity or thought pattern that you would like. The other state is one in which you're feeling too tired or lazy or not motivated [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7acc2h1g1r2jhd7gbbby1a))
- lot of habit formation has to do with being in the right state of mind
  and being able to control your state of body and mind. So [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7aepc9btqwafay3frwkn8w))
- The other key concept for us to address that's really mainly found in the books and articles out there about habits, is this notions of what I call linchpin habits. Linchpin habits are certain habits that make a lot of other habits easier to execute. Now, the sorts of linchpin habits that I'm referring to are always going to be things that you enjoy doing. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7ag8bxn1v3dtxm1ag2xw6a))
- execute. And those linchpin habits always, always, always are things that we enjoy doing [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7akjtw5gseqhx8xdct0px8))
- ask yourself what habits you perform on a daily basis, and these could be things as basic as brushing your teeth before breakfast, or brushing your teeth after breakfast. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7amy5ynsyxajmn8whs5p68))
- Unless we intervene in our habits, they tend to carry out the same way that they always have once they've formed [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7ap60d8esp311mzpp9pwmg))
- Habit strength is measured by two main criteria. The first, is how context-dependent a give habit is [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7apqx2tvjp51cyyrps64vs))
- So it's context independent. So it's a very strong habit, right? [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7aqn6t5jct3s6snvpm2d7d))
- Hebbian learning is when particular neurons are co-active, meaning when they fire together, they tend to strengthen their connections with one another [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mgp4x5xp2kxbkjkkq3gg1))
- So this procedural stepping through of the steps of the recipe or the series of actions steps that are involved in sitting down to study and writing for an hour, or generating exercise, whatever is the habit that you're trying to learn.
  When you're doing that exercise, it's not as if your nervous system thinks you're actually performing that behavior. Your nervous system isn't stupid. It's actually a lot smarter than that. It knows the difference between a thought and an action, but when you do that, it sets in motion the same neurons that are going to be required for the execution of that habit. And so when you actually show up to perform that habit, it's as if the dominoes fall more easily. It's a lower threshold, as we say, in order to get the habit to perform. So, Hebbian learning, NMDA receptors,
  all that nuts and bolts stuff. Really the guts of the mechanisms of how this works. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mjajfg3r0gaspwa2yaq3s))
- The basal ganglia are involved in action execution, meaning doing certain things, and action suppression, not doing certain things. In the experimental realm, these are referred to as go, meaning do, or no go, don't do, certain things. And some of us fall more into the category of we find it very easy to do certain things, but harder to not do other things. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mmf7tmc3jqqtg3nmgx7b2))
- Annual Review of Psychology, excuse me, by Wood and Ruenger [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mmzcx6hhcsm3cnv2wgbpk))
- the dorsolateral striatum is associated, meaning it becomes active at the beginning of a particular habit,
  and at the very end and after a particular habit. Hence the phrase task-bracketing. It brackets the habit [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mp13py0c354qa7z61mm1p))
- other sets of neurons are going to be active during the actual execution of the habit [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mp4ckna4cmjj6ywh19hy1))
- the neural circuits are task bracketing, are deeply embedded in us, meaning they are very robust around a particular habit, well then it's likely that we're going to go out for that zone II cardio no matter what, that we're going to brush our teeth no matter what. I'm fact, brushing our teeth is a pretty good example because, for most people, even if you got a terrible night's sleep, even If everything in your life is going wrong, chances are, unless you're very depressed, if you're going to leave to work, or even if you're not, that you're going to still carry out the behavior [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mq68gcbsfgjrfhehjryny))
- our nervous system tends to generate particular kinds of behavior based not on time, but on our state [[2024-02-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpnk7n0m31358m8axhsjka4x))
- it's not the particular time of day per se that's going to allow you to get into a habit, and form that habit, and consolidate that habit. Rather, it's the state that your brain and body are in that's important to anchor yourself to [[2024-02-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpnk81q0e8s2t6d03act59cz))
- So now I'm going to offer you a tool. It's actually an entire program by which you can insert particular habits and activities at particular phases of the day, not times of day, but phases of the day, because it turns out that particular phases of the day
  are associated with particular biological underpinnings, chemicals and neural circuits and so forth, and in doing so it will make it far more likely that you will be able to regularly engage in these habits and activities over a long period of time [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7mz0rrgqa42e31md19y53z))
- The first is phase one, which is 0 to 8 hours after waking up, approximately. Okay, you can put a plus or minus 30 minutes on this for yourself. The second phase is the 9 to 14, maybe 15 hours
  after you wake up. And the third phase is 16 to 24 hours after waking up. So we've taken the 24 hour cycle, we've carved it up into three phases [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n093hnqcvwkt1sfjeftce))
- most people go to sleep somewhere around 10:00 PM, plus or minus two hours, and wake up some time around 7:00 AM [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n0w3pd0gsezxefp684z4y))
- Regardless of what you do, the neuromodulators norepinephrine as well as epinephrine, so that's noradrenaline and adrenaline, as well as the neuromodulator dopamine tend to be elevated during that first 0 to 8 hours after [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n1d073qmwgfcwdc3pqyqk))
- things that perhaps we should all be doing, I've talked about many of these on the podcast before, that in addition to those chemicals, further support an alert and focused state [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n2tsg5ash0ntd3jz49gv5))
- 30 minutes of waking. Physical exercise of some kind in this phase of the day, 0 to 8 hours of the day, ideally pretty early in that phase, but, you know, if it has to be at the 7 to 8 hour transition point that's fine too. Cold exposure in the form of cold showers or ice baths or outside with minimal clothing, appropriate yet minimal clothing. Caffeine ingestion. Fasting, for instance, not ingesting calories,
  also will lend itself to increased norepinephrine and dopamine, et cetera [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n3gjye8kksvgv01mfk9ba))
- conquer the hardest things first thing in the morning, and that's been discussed in the pop psychology literature and in the habit formation literature merely from the perspective [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n5at6779n7tgadyv5hjd9))
- for 0 to 8 hours, right, in that first phase, your whole system is action and focus oriented. And we know that when you're action and focus oriented [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n5hwb8587xsm9f1cwj08n))
- take the habits for which you know there's the highest degree of limbic friction, they are the hardest for you to engage in, they require the most activation energy, and put those in this 0 to 8 hours after waking. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n67086dmevnck939bak3m))
- by placing them in this broader window of 0 to 8 hours after waking, what you're doing is you're creating task-bracketing.
  You're making it such that your nervous system will predict when you are going to lean in against limbic friction in order to perform particular types of habits. [[2024-02-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpnkbq0gcr4b56cf6xdxy5w9))
- hardest for you to adopt and to maintain and I highly suggest placing those somewhere within this phase one of 0 to 8 hours after waking [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n76ehdegk0smc1vdb0pz3))
- Phase two, as I mentioned, is about, again these aren't specifics, but about 9 to 14 or 15 hours after waking. During this phase of the day, because of the circadian shifts in our biology, the amount of dopamine and norepinephrine that's circulating in our brain and bloodstream tends to start to come down and levels of cortisol tend to start to come down [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7n9eabre9jdzd5qebjvqr2))
- Serotonin is definitely going to be highest in this second half of the day
  and tends to lend itself to a more relaxed state of being [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7na7pdjn8n2egaewmht8k6))
- you want to start tapering off the amount of really bright light that you're getting, [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7nb1yrrb9a23z98k127q2t))
- non-sleep deep rest is an umbrella term for things like meditation, for Yoga Nidra, [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7nbz5c9p0xwtme0h2685k6))
- things like heat and sauna, hot baths, hot showers. Those are terrific things to do in the second half of the day [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7ncjewe395mzds0n70nnrz))
- I caution people about using it for longer than two-week periods of time without taking some breaks. You can look up more about ashwagandha on examine.com. There's a lot of traffic literature with links to studies there. But basically this phase two of the day [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7nd8254nc65hxqnfeyvzkr))
- should say, practicing music regularly, but there's a particular type of music that is hard for you or that you're working on a particular piece of music, or you're trying to learn a language. Something that's a little bit challenging, but doesn't require a ton of energy in order to override that limbic friction.
  The second half of the day is a much better time to do that [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7nfxq22f31ska8ncjf499a))
- set up task-bracketing system so that the individual habits that you're learning
  or that you're trying to learn have a much greater probability of being executed and consolidated, meaning that pretty soon that they will just naturally become reflexive [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm7nj6fnyz0zezxxsbjb37h8))
- the actual rewiring of the brain and the reconfiguration of neurons that will allow that learning to be reflexive, that actually occurs during states of deep rest, like an NSDR and like deep sleep. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm800h9s7362naew2egxaxrg))
- the whole reason for placing particular types of behaviors at particular phases of the day is to set a framework for that task-bracketing. Again, task-bracketing and the circuits of the basal ganglia indicate that it's not just the neural circuits that are engaged by the task itself, but the neural circuits that are engaged before and after that task execution. That's what gets consolidated. So when you do things that particular phases of the day,
  under particular conditions of neurochemistry, what you're doing is you're giving the brain a very predictable set of sequences that during sleep it can start to put into your hard drive, if you will. It can really program it into your nervous system, so that within a short period of time, hopefully within 18 or maybe even 6 days or who knows? Maybe even fewer days, you'll find that executing those behaviors is very, very straightforward for you and that you won't have to feel so much limbic friction
  or override so much limbic friction. Some of you are probably asking, okay, if I perform a particular habit during phase one, and then I do other habits during phase two, and I eventually get to the point where I'm engaging in those habits in a pretty effortless way, do I keep them in the same phase of the day? And the good news is, the literature says it doesn't matter. And in fact, moving that particular habit around somewhat randomly can actually be beneficial to you
  because actually moving it from one time a day to the other is that context independence that we really are seeking. [[2024-01-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8ed2vxbkn69v44dss3fhq0))
- I'll make my own dopamine reward, that's where it comes from. Remember, this is all internal. However, I will also receive a dopamine reward if unexpectedly something positive happens. And typically if something unexpected but positive happens, the amount of dopamine reward that we get is actually much greater than in any other conditions. However, it's hard to surprise yourself about a behavior that you're deliberately engaging [[2024-02-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpq4fzdcjwz1yykyxfjjg2p5))
- Reward prediction error also says that if we expect a reward and the reward doesn't come that the pattern of dopamine release will follow a particular contour and that contour is very important. Here's how it goes [[2024-02-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpq4fhscg82gh8meqtsbmkhr))
- Hey, we're going to go to the amusement park." Or "We're going to go get ice cream." They haven't had the ice cream yet,
  they're not at the amusement park, but they're excited, the dopamine release comes earlier. Okay? They get to the amusement park or they get the ice cream, they will also have some dopamine release associated with that. But most of it, believe it or not, came in the form of the anticipation [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xdxqwhxdmt38tcsjhh28z))
- Now of course, the other way to do is to surprise a kid. You tell them, "Listen, we're going to the class that you absolutely hate."
  Or "We're going to go see the person" "that you absolutely despise." And then you drive them to the amusement park that's the big release of dopamine [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xev0absca5fhbkkqef996))
- Reward prediction error governs virtually all aspects
  of effort and all aspects of learning. Why? Because when dopamine is released in the brain and body, the neural circuits of our brain and body change. There's a state change. Our over level, excuse me, our overall level of energy, but also the sorts of sensory events that we're paying attention to changes when there's a lot of dopamine in our system. Now you can leverage this for habit formation. Think [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xg51k0hvev1h9jrcjm81q))
- you are considering adopting a new habit or if you are trying to break a habit, something we haven't talked too much about, but we will in a moment, it's very useful to think not just about the procedural aspects of what you're going to do, but also think about the events that precede and follow that particular habit, and the execution, or at least the effort to execute that habit [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xh5s2f7ep2c072wmthvra))
- What I should do is positively anticipate the onset and the offset of that session. Right? So thinking about leaning into the effort, going out and doing that zone II cardio session, and I should think about how I'm going to feel after. So, not just thinking about how great I'm going to feel after, but also thinking about how hard it's going to be at the beginning, and then trying to reward myself subjectively for the entire experience. In other words, start rewarding task-bracketing
  in addition to rewarding the execution of the habit itself. Now some of you might be saying, "Well, wait this is all self-talk." "This is just positive self-talk." But it's not positive self-talk, it's not saying, you know, "I feel so great about doing something" "that I actually hate." You can't lie to yourself, or you're welcome to lie to yourself, but the neuroscience literature, the literature of growth mindset, all the literature basically of mindset speaks to the fact that when you lie to yourself, you know you're lying, and you actually set up the opposite of a reward system.
  So, you have to be brutally honest with yourself that, for instance, "I don't like initiating this cardiovascular exercise" "but I do like the fact that I've done it" "after I've done it." So what you are doing is you are applying reward prediction error to the entire sequence of things that's involved in getting into the habit execution, getting through the habit execution, and getting out of the habit of execution. How do you do this? Well, I take us back to our procedural memory visualization exercises we talked about earlier. [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xmbpazy07c2kbtf33snfm))
- I'm drawing a larger envelope around it and starting to positively associate dopamine reward with that larger envelope. And [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xnarhn2br9cmaz4ba17hy))
- pick the habit that you want to form,
  write down or think about very concretely what is the sequence of steps involved in the execution of that habit, and then write down or think about what is the sequence of events that need to precede that habit, maybe the immediate 10 or 15 minutes before, as well as the immediate sequence of events and/or feelings that will occur after that habit [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xtxfes752jzfmgwfz68f3))
- call the whole thing a habit execution [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xv6e9t0qdhm84ykww3c5w))
- dopamine, contrary to popular belief, is not a reward molecule, so much as it is a molecule of motivation and drive [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8xw6q37ycedmsh2g5vkdvb))
- It's the habit of doing a certain number of things per day [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8y2e7g82n1mybkp2fh6sa6))
- there does seem to be something powerful about engaging in particular habits, in a particular sequence, for two days in a row [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8y4b7vcw843q3hg3cggszy))
- how many of those particular habits that I was deliberately trying to learn in the previous 21 days are automatically incorporated into my schedule? How many of them am I naturally doing [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm8y5dtm59bw2ts1fpzbh1s6))

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: The Science of Making & Breaking Habits | Huberman Lab Podcast #53
frontmatter.summary: Habits are reflexive actions that our nervous system learns, and they can either serve us well or undermine our goals. There is a whole field of neuroscience and psychology dedicated to understanding habit formation and breaking. The formation of habits involves biology, such as cells, hormones, and neural pathways. Habits can be strengthened by being specific about when and where they are performed, and by associating reward mechanisms with the entire time frame of the habit. By understanding the neuroscience and psychology of habits, we can better form and break them.

notes.ref: A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface
notes.title: A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpftnsf4hzyksfwawafxjzng))
- When directly cued, habits are represented in memory as direct context–response associations that develop from repeated coacti- vation of the context and response. That is, when the mental representation ofa response (e.g., buckling seatbelt) is consistently activated in conjunction with representation of a context (e.g., getting into a car), associative links gradually form between the two (e.g., buckling seatbelt ⫹ entering a car). [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfvdnmtrq5vb2ga7ewrxd43))
- o (e.g., buckling seatbelt ⫹ entering a car). The essential mechanism behind direct cuing involves the cog- nitive neural changes that result from repeated coactivation of responses and contexts (see Hebb, 1949). [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfvemqce7wp06hddbyrayzp))
- With repetition, incre- mental changes occur in relevant processors or neural assemblies in procedural memory, essentially tuning the processing elements in ways that facilitate the repeated aspects of responding to recur- ring features of performance contexts. [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfvx0n81epftc1zn2sgfk6m))
- . Given sufficient repetition, the cues themselves then carry power to motivate the response because they signal an opportunity to acquire the associated reward. I [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfvks4b62n6wcrme68y9vgv))

frontmatter.author: Wendy Wood and David T. Neal
frontmatter.title: A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface
frontmatter.summary: This article explores the mechanisms underlying habitual control of responding and the ways in which habits interface with goals. It suggests that goals can direct habits by motivating repetition and promoting exposure to cues that trigger habits. Habits, once acquired, can be performed without the mediation of a goal. The article also discusses the role of context cues in activating habit responses and the variability in habit performance depending on the availability or accessibility of explicit goals. Overall, the article highlights the complex relationship between habits and goals in shaping human behavior.

notes.ref: 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail
notes.title: 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- PROBLEM 1: Trying to Change Everything at Once
  **SOLUTION:** Pick one thing and do it well. [[2024-01-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm5cmwcvd4pxna8cf28knaeq))
- The highest number you’ll find is changing three habits at once and that suggestion comes from BJ Fogg at Stanford University. Let’s be clear: Dr. Fogg is talking about incredibly tiny habits.
  How tiny? His suggested habits include flossing one tooth, doing one pushup per day, or saying “It’s going to be a great day” when you get out of bed in the morning. So, even if you keep your new habits that small, you should work on no more than three habits at a time. (Sidenote: I think BJ Fogg’s [Tiny Habits program](http://tinyhabits.com/) is great. I believe it is also free, so you can’t beat the price.) [[2024-01-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm5cnz6zjvdjjns6eyze475p))
- When in doubt, pick something that could potentially be a keystone habit. [[2024-01-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm5cps4ns14m2tqfpk1terp6))
- the most difficult part of a new habit is starting the behavior [[2024-01-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm5crcebfd7hjjmpan9jfbcm))
- If you want a new habit, you have to fall in love with a new ritual. [[2024-01-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm5csaakt80njf5pb4a0sq6m))

frontmatter.author: James Clear
frontmatter.title: 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail
frontmatter.summary: Around 81 to 92 percent of New Year's resolutions fail, indicating that it is challenging to stick to new habits. The article highlights five common mistakes that cause new habits to fail and provides solutions for each problem. These mistakes include trying to change everything at once, starting with a habit that is too big, seeking a result rather than focusing on the behavior, not changing the environment, and assuming that small changes don't add up. The article emphasizes the importance of focusing on one habit at a time, starting with small and manageable habits, and creating an environment that promotes good habits.

notes.ref: We Are the Art Brandon Sanderson’s Keynote Speech
notes.title: We Are the Art | Brandon Sanderson’s Keynote Speech
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/We Are the Art Brandon Sanderson’s Keynote Speech.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Like a lot of things, we can look to Star Trek for help. [cheering] I am a nerd after all. So, my Star Trek
  was Next Generation. I'd stay up late at night and I'd watch it because it was at 11:00 and my mom would want me to turn off my TV because it was 11 o'clock Sunday. So, there was school the next day and I would not cuz I was watching Star Trek. Got to watch my Star Trek. In Star Trek Next Generation, we got Data, right? He's an android and a lot of his character arcs were about exploring what it means to be human. One of the recurring themes in the show was his attempts to create art. Painting, poetry, music, become a comedian. Take
  my warf, please. Classic line. I rooted for data, a synthetic being without emotions, trying so hard to understand the human experience. And I still do. I have no problem with data creating art. If he were real, I'd applaud him. Why do I empathize with data yet not the AI large language models? This question, this starts to get to the core of the issue for me. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtv9beh8gmnfkwhb6xt0qdb))
- I do think that part of the reason I dislike AI is because it is too focused on the product and not the process. Yes, the message is journey before destination. It is always journey before destination, but there's a specific take on it this time. This is a page in my first book. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvgqmb6rm4552v6zaq5mc7))
- Maybe someday the language modules will be able to write books better than I can. But here's the thing, using those models in such a way absolutely misses the point because it looks at art only as a product. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvh5kj9qhxt88sfkj6z7sw))
- I tell you right now, if you've never finished a project on this level, it's one of the most sweet and beautiful and transcendent moments in my life was holding that manuscript, thinking to myself, I did it. I did it. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvhfzrrtdaq4wys61wxhjw))
- Data created art because he wanted to grow. He wanted
  to become something. He wanted to understand art is the means by which we become what we want to be. The purpose of writing all those books in my earlier years wasn't to produce something I could sell. It was to turn me into someone who could create great art. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvhx05y7rbnz4ytybssx4v))
- . I think this is why I rebel against the AI art product so much because they steal the opportunity for for growth from us. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvjgz8kg19nm520ry07yqg))
    - Note: Wow
- Writing a prompt for an LLM, even refining what it spits out, will not make an artist of you. Because if you haven't done the hard part, if you haven't watched a book spiral completely out of control, if you haven't written something you thought was wonderful and then had readers get completely lost because your narrative chops aren't strong enough, if you haven't beat your head against the wall of dead ends on a story day after day until you break it down and find the unexpected path, you're not going to
  have the skill to refine that prompt. The machine will have done the hard part for you and it doesn't care. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvd26vpaeh2xfn4m2khb76))
- For it was created to try to make something useful, but it cannot admire what it made. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvddpmzp113j13qmpx2jpv))
- What is art? Art is what we
  define it to be. Why do we make art? Well, remember art is not just the story. It is not just the painting or the sculpture or whatever else you love to create. It's also the process of creation and what that process did to you. [[2026-02-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgtvepvsqzdc9wce8ep18c00))

frontmatter.author: Brandon Sanderson
frontmatter.title: We Are the Art | Brandon Sanderson’s Keynote Speech
frontmatter.summary: Brandon Sanderson says art is not just the final product but the process that changes the artist. He worries AI art misses this human growth and connection. In the end, we are the true art because creating changes who we become.

frontmatter.author: Big Think
frontmatter.title: 1177 BC: The Vanishing of the First Globalized World | Eric Cline: Full Interview
frontmatter.summary: Around 1177 BC, the ancient Mediterranean world collapsed quickly, ending many international connections and empires like the Hittites. Some societies, like the Phoenicians, adapted and created new systems, while others, like the Mycenaeans, had to rebuild from scratch. This collapse shows how resilience can shape the future and offers lessons for us today.

notes.ref: I’d Rather Re-Read These 10 Books Over and Over Again Instead of Starting New Ones
notes.title: I’d Rather Re-Read These 10 Books Over and Over Again Instead of Starting New Ones
notes.body:
## Highlights
- On the Shortness of Life by Seneca [[2024-02-17]] 
- This book has a simple thesis: Life is long if you know how to live it. It talks about how life seems short because we waste so much of it. [[2024-02-17]] 
- Most things will be okay eventually, but not everything will be. [[2024-02-17]] 
- Don’t do what you know on a gut level to be the wrong thing to do [[2024-02-17]] 
- I don’t think there’s a single dumbass thing I’ve done in my adult life that I didn’t know was a dumbass thing to do while I was doing i [[2024-02-17]] 
- Definite optimism — Definite optimists have a long-term plan for the future they execute and never waver from. Steve Jobs comes to mind, creating the iPod, iPad, and iPhone over the span of a decade. This goes against the thinking that you should build a minimum viable product and “iterate your way to success.” [[2024-02-17]] 
- Answer this question — What important truth do you disagree with most people on? [[2024-02-17]] 
- Spend each day trying to be a little wiser than you were when you woke up. Discharge your duties faithfully and well. Systematically you get ahead, but not necessarily in fast spurts. Nevertheless, you build discipline by preparing for fast spurts. Slug it out one inch at a time, day by day. At the end of the day — if you live long enough — most people get what they d [[2024-02-17]] 
- Invert, invert, invert — Instead of trying to be smart, avoid being dumb. Instead of trying to make amazing decisions, avoid catastrophic ones. [[2024-02-17]] 
- Deserve what you want — To get what you want, you have to be the type of person who deserves it. Re-read that. Simple and profound. [[2024-02-17]] 
- “Read what you love until you love to read.” — Naval. [[2024-02-17]]

notes.title: How to Start Managing Your Energy Levels Instead of Your Time
notes.body:
## Highlights
- Working on creative tasks, making decisions and seeing the larger picture are all easy in these high-energy times. [[2024-01-15]] 
- For high-energy levels: creative work (coming up with novel ideas), creative team discussions, making difficult or high-risk decisions, planning for mid-term and long-term, actively learn new content, make presentations and pitches, thinking and reflecting, experimenting and trying new things, writing to-do lists [[2024-01-15]] 
- For low-energy levels: routine work, maintenance and cleaning, working through tasks that have clear goals and processes, writing correspondence and emails, reviewing what you learned, writing invoices. [[2024-01-15]] 
- For relaxing levels: eating, sleeping, sports, outdoor activities, meditation, social times, gaming, shopping, movies etc. [[2024-01-15]] 
- Another important factor is to look at your habits and shift them around if neccessary. One example: commutes are often seen as “dead time”, but might in fact be a golden opportunity to do high-energy work. If you are in your car, you have space to think and could use voice dictation to get ideas down. On a train, the time could be spend reading and learning — depending on how you feel. [[2024-01-15]]

notes.ref: Wendy Wood
notes.title: Wendy Wood
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Wendy Wood.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- People are most likely to form habits when contexts promote easy repetition and when the behavior itself is rewarding.[[5](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wendy_Wood_(psychologist)#cite_note-5) [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfv3hpfx1x4qshqckmeg41h))
- When people experience changes in everyday contexts, such as when they move house or start a new job, then their old behaviors are no longer automatically cued. Context changes thus disrupt automatic repetition and force people to make decisions. Unless they have strong intentions to continue the behavior, they are unlikely to do so.[[4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wendy_Wood_(psychologist)#cite_note-Wood-4) [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfv026pgff4g5ysxxe9zqjh))

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Wendy Wood
frontmatter.summary: Wendy Wood is a UK-born psychologist and Provost Professor of Psychology and Business at the University of Southern California. Her primary research focuses on habits and behavior change, as well as the psychology of gender. Wood is the author of the book "Good Habits, Bad Habits" and has received numerous awards for her research. She has also made influential contributions in the study of sex and gender, as well as attitudes and social influence. Wood's research has been widely recognized and cited in the field of psychology.

notes.ref: Johnny.Decimal
notes.title: Johnny.Decimal
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Johnny.Decimal.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- We take it for granted that we can usually use our browser history or some sort of 'recent files' list to find what we want. And this often works. [[2025-11-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kbbahq5nw28wqna3tqg12znc))
- **14.01 Go for walks**
  I don't want to tell you how to work ... but I'm going to.
  This task is well suited to a good walk. You want to let your mind wander, to allow it to consider the broader picture. I find even just walking round the block sparks inspiration – far more so than sitting staring at a screen, anyway.
  Your phone will have a way to record a 'voice memo'. Search online to find out how. I use this all the time when I'm walking, and transcribe my notes when I get back home. [[2025-12-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kc9spsse3vk5k5w8tgm6hxwt))
- **33.03 Home > Insurance, or Insurance > Home?**
  Doing it both ways might lead to different results; as in, totally different areas and categories. Remember, there isn't a 'right' answer here. This is more art than science.
  Take something like your home insurance. Do you classify that as 'Home > Insurance' – that is, 'Insurance' as a category under the area of 'Home' – or 'Insurance > Home'?
  It depends! It depends on your brain; it depends on the rest of your system; it depends on whether you're an insurance broker or a real estate agent. And that's cool. Either will work. [[2025-11-28]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb70zgy2fsejh5sk022shn3e))
- **42.02 Areas and categories are containers**
  One of the important limitations of the system is that we don't store anything in area or category folders. This enforces structure and prevents chaos. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb8a7av5dm550rdtpwd86js7))
- We say that your Johnny.Decimal structure is 'only two levels deep', but it's more than that: everything you save is *exactly* two levels deep. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb8a7pypx1s0sb7rq0bzks6b))
- In order to save anything – a file, a note, an email – we need to create an ID within a category. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb8a82n2d24v21rb3ewe8v5v))
- It will usually be one of the higher numbers because recent things have higher numbers. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb8ssyavpzg9gmddfq5mp4pa))
- **44.02 You really create a folder to save one thing?**
  People wonder if I really create a *whole folder* to save just one thing. They think I'm mad. I think they're lazy. How much time does it take to create a folder? No time at all. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb8stskhevqzqw4jmwsy5sa9))
- And then you realise – *aah, it's in my email!*
  This confusion is real. It causes us to lose focus. It causes us to resent the computer; rather than sitting down and facing a tranquil ocean, we face a raging sea. This is turning in to a self-help book but it's true! Your computer needs to be a happy place. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb9ev79c5056aaw5xjp51stm))
- And then you realise – *aah, it's in my email!*
  This confusion is real. It causes us to lose focus. It causes us to resent the computer; rather than sitting down and facing a tranquil ocean, we face a raging sea. This is turning in to a self-help book but it's true! Your computer needs to be a happy place. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb9ewpesbq7f3e0wbmy8hpgd))
- Recording the structure of your system [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb9f647bqw9jwz4e8ts7sr5r))
- Given that creation of areas and categories is rare, we can solve this by creating a note with the structure of our system. We shouldn't need to update it very often. [[2025-11-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb9f5vdmtdrsde11c7pgsp0g))

frontmatter.author: Johnny.Decimal
frontmatter.title: Johnny.Decimal
frontmatter.summary: Disorganisation happens when you don't know where to start looking. Use a few broad folders so each holds many related items and you can find things easily. Keep the system small and focused so it stays manageable.

frontmatter.author: Mark Manson

notes.ref: LLM-Wiki · GitHub
notes.title: LLM-Wiki · GitHub
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/LLM-Wiki · GitHub.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- This is the key difference: **the wiki is a persistent, compounding artifact.** The cross-references are already there. The contradictions have already been flagged. The synthesis already reflects everything you've read. The wiki keeps getting richer with every source you add and every question you ask.
  You never (or rarely) write the wiki yourself — the LLM writes and maintains all of it. You're in charge of sourcing, exploration, and asking the right questions. The LLM does all the grunt work — the summarizing, cross-referencing, filing, and bookkeeping that makes a knowledge base actually useful over time. In practice, I have the LLM agent open on one side and Obsidian open on the other. The LLM makes edits based on our conversation, and I browse the results in real time — following links, checking the graph view, reading the updated pages. Obsidian is the IDE; the LLM is the programmer; the wiki is the codebase.
  This can apply to a lot of different contexts. A few examples:
  • **Personal**: tracking your own goals, health, psychology, self-improvement — filing journal entries, articles, podcast notes, and building up a structured picture of yourself over time.
  • **Research**: going deep on a topic over weeks or months — reading papers, articles, reports, and incrementally building a comprehensive wiki with an evolving thesis.
  • **Reading a book**: filing each chapter as you go, building out pages for characters, themes, plot threads, and how they connect. By the end you have a rich companion wiki. Think of fan wikis like [Tolkien Gateway](https://tolkiengateway.net/wiki/Main_Page) — thousands of interlinked pages covering characters, places, events, languages, built by a community of volunteers over years. You could build something like that personally as you read, with the LLM doing all the cross-referencing and maintenance.
  • **Business/team**: an internal wiki maintained by LLMs, fed by Slack threads, meeting transcripts, project documents, customer calls. Possibly with humans in the loop reviewing updates. The wiki stays current because the LLM does the maintenance that no one on the team wants to do.
  • **Competitive analysis, due diligence, trip planning, course notes, hobby deep-dives** — anything where you're accumulating knowledge over time and want it organized rather than scattered. [[2026-04-19]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kpja6k8xknggqkrh7hnz6rw6))

frontmatter.title: LLM-Wiki · GitHub
frontmatter.summary: The llm-wiki is a personal knowledge base where an AI reads and summarizes new sources into a growing, linked wiki. This wiki updates itself with new information and keeps everything consistent over time. You guide the AI by adding sources and asking questions, while it does the hard work of organizing and synthesizing knowledge.

notes.ref: Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX
notes.title: Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- The movement time to a target depends on the size of the target and the distance to the target. [[2024-01-27]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hn69bj5htbx9cqsg5bv92f65))
- make targets big [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpg9zwe4jgp979w2gmkrvr1s))
- Not only does a text label reduce icon ambiguity and makes it easy to understand, but it also improves movement time to that particular target [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpg9zm5w0h4hj96pwhdgyg60))
- make the entire area clickable and avoid the design mistake of only making the icon itself the active target [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpg9zae7saw4zysav11s8jqt))
- a study by Daniel Avrahami showed that, for touchscreen devices, it actually takes longer to hit targets placed around the edges, possibly because for touchscreens, it is possible to overshoot and hit beyond the edge of the screen. [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpg9vv4e9q7bk5kr2pjemvja))
- The average distance from menu handle to a menu element depends on the type of menu: linear menus are less efficient than rectangular menus, which, in turn, are less efficient than pie menus. [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpga6vwqqbm5ynp13wepax4g))
- If you know that a set of controls will be clicked in a certain order, [place them close to each other](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/closeness-of-actions-and-objects-gui/) (but not too close [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpga99yqxjz1pc5xyvdfmwrg))
- Fitts’s law says that the time to move to a target depends on how big it is and on how far away it is. As you are creating new UI designs, think about optimizing both these variables by creating well-spaced, big targets and positioning them so that they are close to the user’s most probable prior location. [[2024-02-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hprbgxksb0ak18aq73ykng8p))

frontmatter.author: Raluca Budiu
frontmatter.title: Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX
frontmatter.summary: Fitts's Law is a principle that states the movement time to a target depends on the size of the target and the distance to the target. It was developed by psychologist Paul Fitts, who studied the relationship between human movement and design. Fitts's Law has implications for user experience (UX) design, particularly in terms of optimizing target size and distance to target. The law suggests that larger targets and closer targets are easier and faster to acquire. Designers can apply Fitts's Law by making targets bigger, using icons with labels, avoiding crowding targets, and placing related targets close to each other.

notes.ref: Choose a Highlight to Make Time Every Day
notes.title: Choose a Highlight to Make Time Every Day
notes.body:
## Highlights
- focusing on activities that fall between long-term goals and short-term tasks is the key to slowing down, bringing satisfaction to your daily life, and helping you make time [[2022-07-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1523628052/20143260))
- If, at the end of the day, someone asks you, “What was the highlight of your day?” what do you want your answer to be? [[2022-07-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1523628052/20143264))
- You can often find urgent Highlights on your to-do list, email, or calendar — look for projects that are time-sensitive, important, and medium in size (in other words, they don’t take 10 minutes, but they don’t take 10 hours, either). [[2022-07-25]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1523628052/20185816))
- Your urgent Highlight might be one of the following:
  Create a price quote and send it to a customer who’s expecting it before the end of the week.
  Request catering and venue proposals for an event you’re organizing at work.
  Prepare and cook dinner before friends come over.
  Help your daughter finish a big school project that’s due tomorrow.
  Edit and share vacation photos that your family is eager to see. [[2022-07-25]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1523628052/20185825))

## Full Document

## Highlights
- But there's also something in here about our tendency to forget or skim over all the potential richness of our everyday experience. [[2026-03-01]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kjp6nfhm9awt6j53rvg4w54y))
- My point is that inhabiting Tolken's mind has temporarily made the world a bit more beautiful and it's forced me to take a familiar site, the
  trees outside my window that I see literally every day and make them strange again. Many of us think that at some level being present or being in the moment is a desirable thing. But this is often reduced to a thought-terminating cliche unless we're given a detailed idea of how to actually be present or what this state can consist of. And sure, there are ways of training this directly. Meditation is one of the most popular. But another way to approach
  this conundrum is to figure out a way to make the present moment itself more compelling. And one way to do this is to make it strange and so recapture this sense of novelty. It is taking something mundane and making it new again. [[2026-03-01]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kjpf2jn2666rjean8vcxn540))

frontmatter.author: arena-attachments.s3.amazonaws.com
frontmatter.title: Feynman on memory and triangulation
frontmatter.summary: In this document, physicist Richard Feynman provides tips on studying physics. He emphasizes the importance of understanding the interrelationships of nature rather than simply memorizing formulas. Feynman encourages students to "triangulate" from what is known to what is unknown, using logic and known facts to fill in gaps in knowledge. He explains that as long as one knows enough and doesn't forget too much, it is possible to continually recreate forgotten information. Feynman concludes that the real utility of physicists lies in discovering new laws and making new discoveries by triangulating from the known to the previously unknown.

notes.ref: A system to organise your life
notes.title: A system to organise your life
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/A system to organise your life.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Step 1: Buy ten shelves
  Imagine a computer is a garage. We can't put everything on the floor, so we buy ten shelves. Then we dedicate each one to an area of our life -- `life admin`, `home business`, and `tennis club`.[1](https://johnnydecimal.com/?ueid=7cf87940f82f55837513bff273e1c65f&utm_source=Sailthru&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=2024-08-31%2520Installer%252050&utm_term=Installer#user-content-fn-room-to-grow)
  ![](https://johnnydecimal.com/img/v6/11.01C-Berkeley_shelves_x3--0_original-light-cx-1312x918.png)
  FIGURE 11.01C. A SHELF FOR EACH MAJOR AREA OF OUR LIFE.
  Step 2: Add some boxes
  Each shelf has space for ten boxes, so we categorise what we want to store. In life admin we decide on five and label them: `me`, `house`, `money`, `online`, and `travel`. Our boxes have space for a number, so we add that too.[2](https://johnnydecimal.com/?ueid=7cf87940f82f55837513bff273e1c65f&utm_source=Sailthru&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=2024-08-31%2520Installer%252050&utm_term=Installer#user-content-fn-startat11)
  ![](https://johnnydecimal.com/img/v6/11.01D-Berkeley_shelf_w_boxes--0_original-light-cx-448x914.png)
  FIGURE 11.01D: OUR LIFE ADMIN SHELF ENDS UP WITH FIVE BOXES.
  Step 3: File your stuff in folders
  We put our documents in manila folders. Each folder gets a number starting at `.11` so we can track them. In this case, we've put some insurance policies in `15.23 Travel insurance`. Then put the folder in a box. [[2025-11-28]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb6jqtebj7hyses8r6dwgjxq))
- Benefits of the Johnny.Decimal ID
  Each of our storage folders now has a number, the ID. It always has two digits, a decimal, and two digits. For example, `15.23` `22.11` `31.17`. This number is really useful.
  It provides structure
  The ID tells us exactly where a thing is. The numbers before the decimal are the item's category, and they define the structure of your system. [[2025-11-28]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kb6k22h08x0j8dyrzc26mczq))

frontmatter.author: johnnydecimal.com
frontmatter.title: A system to organise your life
frontmatter.summary: The Johnny.Decimal system helps you organize your life by assigning unique IDs to everything, making it easier to find items and stay organized. It uses a simple structure with areas, categories, and numbers to keep your files neat and accessible. This system is free to use at home, work, or in clubs, and encourages limited choices to reduce chaos.
