notes.ref: Psychology of Habit
notes.title: Psychology of Habit
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Psychology of Habit.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- [William James's (1890)](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-psych-122414-033417#) view that “habit covers a very large part of life,” necessitated that we “define clearly just what its limits are” (p. 104) [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfth82azv5z8fnvxabkhae2))
- Habits strengthen through associative and reward-learning mechanisms that capture the slow, incremental nature of habit formation. With each repetition, small changes occur in the cognitive and neural mechanisms associated with procedural memory. Through Hebbian learning, cognitive associations between context cues and a response are strengthened gradually so that people are prepared to repeat performance when the context cues are encountered again (called direct cuing by [Wood & Neal 2007](https://www.annualreviews.org/doi/10.1146/annurev-psych-122414-033417#)). [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpftkq0d1yh8w2prsfv6qgr3))

frontmatter.author: Annual Reviews
frontmatter.title: Psychology of Habit
frontmatter.summary: Habits are characterized by their cognitive, motivational, and neurobiological properties. They form as people repeat actions in a given context while pursuing goals and become the default mode of response. Habits are influenced by associative and reward-learning mechanisms and are activated by recurring context cues. Computational models explain the cognitive processes involved in habit learning and performance, highlighting the interaction between habits and deliberate goal pursuit. The neurobiology of habits involves the sensorimotor cortico-basal ganglia loop, and multiple brain regions participate in both habitual and goal-directed control. Factors such as stress and learning mechanisms can shift the balance between habits and deliberate goal pursuit. People often infer underlying motives from the frequency of habit performance, even though habits are not necessarily goal-driven.

notes.ref: WikipediaNotability - Wikipedia
notes.title: Wikipedia:Notability - Wikipedia
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/WikipediaNotability - Wikipedia.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- We require "significant coverage" in reliable sources so that we can actually write a whole article, rather than half a paragraph or a [definition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Wikipedia_is_not_a_dictionary) of that topic. If only a few sentences could be written and supported by sources about the subject, that subject does not qualify for a separate page, but should instead be [merged](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Merging) into an article about a larger topic or relevant list. (See [the advice below](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:FAILN).) [[2024-07-04]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j20cy8dsmkeppvh6vf5tp2js))
- There are numerous reasons for these requirements. [[2024-07-04]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j20df6t5fr7s7a2gyfrzc8n3))
- Wikipedia is not and must not become the validating source for non-significant subjects, and it is not a forum for [original research](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research).[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wikipedia:Notability&diffonly=true#cite_note-7) For writers and editors of Wikipedia articles to write about controversial ideas in a [neutral manner](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view), it is of vital importance that they simply restate what is said by independent [secondary sources](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Primary,_secondary,_and_tertiary_sources#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources) of reasonable reliability and quality. [[2024-07-04]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j20dfag0tzjz4028a4fmeygc))

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Wikipedia:Notability - Wikipedia
frontmatter.summary: Information on Wikipedia must be verifiable; if no reliable, independent sources can be found on a topic, then it should not have a separate article.

notes.ref: Free Will Is as Real as Baseball
notes.title: Free Will Is as Real as Baseball
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Free Will Is as Real as Baseball.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- The consequence argument points out that deterministic laws imply that the future isn’t *really* up for grabs; it’s determined by the present state just as surely as the past is. So we don’t really have choices about anything. [[2024-04-07]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01htvpzagq0ss6ybe6yfnh9ygw))
- Given our lack of complete microscopic information, the question we should be asking is, “does the best theory of human beings include an element of free choice?” The reason why it might is precisely because we have different epistemic access to the past and the future. The low entropy of the past allows for the existence of “records” and “memories,” and consequently forces us to model the past as “settled.” We have no such restriction toward the future, which is why we model the future as something we can influence. [[2024-04-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01htvnbwm19nymyrwr444665w8))
- From this perspective, free will is no more ruled out by the consequence argument than the Second Law of Thermodynamics is ruled out by microscopic reversibility. [[2024-04-07]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01htvq0t5m4dmck4ekft7fge9h))

frontmatter.author: preposterousuniverse.com
frontmatter.title: Free Will Is as Real as Baseball
frontmatter.summary: The text discusses different perspectives on the existence of free will, considering it as an emergent phenomenon compatible with a materialist view of the world. It highlights the tension between microscopic views based on particles and macroscopic views where choices are made, suggesting that the concept of free will remains a useful model for understanding human behavior. The text ultimately questions whether free will is a necessary component in the best theory of human behavior and rational decision-making.

frontmatter.author: Cal Newport
frontmatter.title: Is the Internet Making You Lazy?
frontmatter.summary: The internet can distract us and lead our ambition toward unhelpful or extreme goals. True excellence comes from simple, meaningful routines, not complicated or flashy ones. To reclaim ambition, we must focus deeply on what matters and reduce digital distractions in our work.

notes.ref: Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form
notes.title: Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Although we may think of each of these routines as a single task, they are usually made up of many smaller actions, such as picking up our toothbrush, squeezing toothpaste onto it, and then lifting the brush to our mouth. This process of grouping behaviors together into a single routine is known as “chunking,” [[2024-02-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpnht9az7kgycfs22v52rce6))
- However, these previous studies did not rule out other explanations for the pattern, including the possibility that it might be related to the motor commands required for the maze-running behavior. In the new study, Martiros and Graybiel set out to determine whether this firing pattern could be conclusively linked with the chunking of habitual behavior.
  The researchers trained rats to press two levers in a particular sequence, for example, 1-2-2 or 2-1-2. The rats had to figure out what the correct sequence was, and if they did, they received a chocolate milk reward. It took several weeks for them to learn the task, and as they became more accurate, the researchers saw the same beginning-and-end firing patterns develop in the striatum that they had seen in their previous habit studies.
  Because each rat learned a different sequence, the researchers could rule out the possibility that the patterns correspond to the motor input required to preform a particular series of movements. This offers strong evidence that the firing pattern corresponds specifically to the initiation and termination of a learned routine, the researchers say.
  “I think this more or less proves that the development of bracketing patterns serves to package up a behavior that the brain — and the animals — consider valuable and worth keeping in their repertoire. It really is a high-level signal that helps to release that habit, and we think the end signal says the routine has been done,” Graybiel says. [[2024-02-14]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfxq18zmz6yn2s6w49d176d))
- Graybiel has previously shown that a part of the brain called the striatum, which is found in the basal ganglia, plays a major role in habit formation. Several years ago, she and her group found that neuron firing patterns in the striatum change as animals learn a new habit, such as turning to the right or left in a maze upon hearing a certain tone.
  When the animal is just starting to learn the maze, these neurons fire continuously throughout the task. However, as the animal becomes better at making the correct turn to receive a reward, the firing becomes clustered at the very beginning of the task and at the very end. Once these patterns form, it becomes extremely difficult to break the habit. [[2024-02-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hpfxkfryxz52ftcxw0qf98pc))

frontmatter.author: Anne Trafton
frontmatter.title: Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form
frontmatter.summary: MIT neuroscientists have discovered that certain neurons in the brain are responsible for marking the beginning and end of routine behaviors, a process known as "chunking." These neurons fire at the start of a learned routine, go quiet during the routine, and then fire again once the routine is complete. This task-bracketing is important for initiating and completing routines. The researchers trained rats to perform a particular sequence of lever presses and observed the same firing patterns in the rats' brains as they had seen in previous habit studies. The study provides evidence that the firing pattern corresponds specifically to the initiation and termination of a learned routine.

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Ann Graybiel
frontmatter.summary: Ann Martin Graybiel is a renowned neuroscientist and an expert on the basal ganglia. Her research has focused on the physiology of the striatum and its role in habit formation, implicit learning, and various neurological disorders. Graybiel's discoveries have shed light on the neural mechanisms underlying Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, and obsessive-compulsive disorder. She has received numerous awards and recognition for her contributions to neuroscience, including the National Medal of Science and the Kavli Prize. Graybiel is a member of prestigious scientific organizations and continues to make significant contributions to the field of neuroscience.

[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Fitts's Law.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- The first [human–computer interface](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human%E2%80%93computer_interface) application of Fitts's law was by Card, English, and Burr,[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fitts's_law#cite_note-Card1978-11) who used the index of performance (*IP*), interpreted as 1⁄*b*, to compare performance of different [input devices](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Input_device), with the [mouse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mouse_(computing)) coming out on top compared to the joystick or directional movement keys.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fitts's_law#cite_note-Card1978-11) This early work, according to [Stuart Card](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stuart_Card)'s biography, "was a major factor leading to the mouse's commercial introduction by [Xerox](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xerox)".[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fitts's_law#cite_note-12) [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0ekqd9arhcjefbrdr3bk66))
- A movement during a single Fitts's law task can be split into two phases:[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fitts's_law#cite_note-Graham-10)
  • *initial movement*. A fast but imprecise movement towards the target
  • *final movement*. Slower but more precise movement in order to acquire the target [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0emtr90vz7s80vsde7473h))
- the difficulty of a pointing task was equated to a quantity of information transmitted (in units of bits) by performing the task [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0eq4tez61e2q2nrfq6dtaf))
- In its original form, Fitts's law is meant to apply only to one-dimensional tasks. [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0es7mnhkf1fwfke42axvh4))
- A major application for Fitts's law is 2D virtual pointing tasks on computer screens, in which targets have bounded sizes in both dimensions. [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0esx3vw6ca4ma211mzzjtk))
- Multiple design guidelines for [GUIs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GUI) can be derived from the implications of Fitts's law. In its basic form, Fitts's law says that targets a user has to hit should be as big as possible. This is derived from the *W* parameter. [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0ew4t3nsa2gc3zz6fyq767))
- Layouts should also cluster functions that are commonly used with each other. Optimizing for the *D* parameter in this way allows for smaller travel times. [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0ex55f5bn089hw8h6jbg8p))
- Placing layout elements on the four edges of the screen allows for infinitely large targets in one dimension and therefore presents ideal scenarios. Since the pointer will always stop at the edge, the user can move the mouse with the greatest possible speed and still hit the target. [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0ezhg7dt5mmwf1vpgc46q1))
- this guideline is called “Rule of the infinite edges”. The use of this rule can be seen for example in [MacOS](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MacOS), which always places the menu bar on the top left edge of the screen instead of the current program's windowframe.[[30]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fitts's_law#cite_note-Hale2007-30) [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0ezr8512yvvfhgy9dxrpk4))
- A UI that allows for pop-up menus rather than fixed drop-down menus reduces travel times for the *D* parameter. The user can continue interaction right from the current mouse position and doesn't have to move to a different preset area. [[2024-01-12]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm0f1pvk0249rma3qf4rn8nx))

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Fitts's Law
frontmatter.summary: Fitts's law is a predictive model used in human-computer interaction and ergonomics to determine the time required to move to a target area. It states that the time is based on the ratio of the distance to the target and the width of the target. The law is used to model pointing tasks, both physical and virtual. There have been various formulations and extensions of the law, including adjustments for accuracy and incorporating information theory. Fitts's law has provided insights into user interface design principles and can be used as a performance metric for interfaces.

notes.ref: Why I'm Leaving Medium - Forte Labs
notes.title: Why I'm Leaving Medium - Forte Labs
notes.body:
## Highlights
- I don’t think there’s anyone more invested in the success of Medium than I am. And over the next week I’ll be taking my writing and my audience to a new WordPress blog (the one you’re reading now). [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622613))
- But around that same time I started reading Stratechery, Ben Thompson’s email newsletter offering “analysis of the strategy and business side of technology and media.” [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622651))
- this contradicted everything I thought I knew about media. I don’t think there is a topic more oversaturated than tech news and analysis, and here was one man single-handedly producing the best content in the whole industry. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622654))
- For the first time in the internet age, it now makes economic sense to focus on a specific niche, write only high-quality content that appeals to that niche, and monetize the audience yourself using subscriptions and information products, rather than relying on advertising. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622665))
- a subscriber is paying for the regular delivery of well-defined value. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622670))
- First, because I won’t make anywhere close to the revenue I’m making now. Why would I give up $5 per member per month in exchange for random readers that I’ll probably never see again? [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622696))
- I have 8,000 followers, but the only ones I can contact directly are the 400+ who pay for my publication. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622700))
- Because digital reading is seen in terms of utility, not entertainment, people don’t want more volume, diversity, or access. They don’t want “unlimited reading.” Reading is hard work. People don’t like hard work, so they won’t pay you to assign more of it to them. They want to read as little as they can get away with! [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622709))
- What they care most about is an easy writing experience, and readership. It is exhilarating as a casual blogger to see hundreds or thousands of people reading what you’ve written. I think a lot of this writing is migrating to Facebook, where it’s even easier and more discoverable. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622712))
- You no longer need to scale massively to build a profitable business. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622715))
- The option of focusing intently on a core audience is enabling the rise of “online small businesses” that never need to wade into the storm of mass media. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19622716))
- To be clear, I believe Medium is still the absolute best place to start writing for the vast majority of people. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19624159))
- Most people spend so much of their initial motivation “designing the furniture” — picking themes, customizing templates, fiddling with settings, worrying about layout — that they never actually make a habit of writing. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19624160))
- Medium will continue to capture the high end of this group, but not behind its paywall. If all you care about is people reading what you’ve written, why restrict access? [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19624161))
- It seems the only ones left are part-time self-improvement gurus. [[2022-05-21]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1508264123/19624557))

frontmatter.title: Why I'm Leaving Medium - Forte Labs

notes.ref: Wikipedia Is the Last Best Place on the Internet
notes.title: Wikipedia Is the Last Best Place on the Internet
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Wikipedia Is the Last Best Place on the Internet.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- The heroes of Wikipedia are not giants in their fields but so-called WikiGnomes—editors who sweep up typos, arrange articles in neatly categorized piles, and scrub away vandalism. This work is often thankless, but it does not seem to be joyless. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w8wwp5915j2d35m79sd8pd))
- As with the law, there are different schools of interpretation; the two largest of these are deletionists and inclusionists. Deletionists favor quality over quantity, and notability over utility. Inclusionists are the opposite. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w8xv78zjqap0110v5v1bwq))
- But while accuracy binds the trust between reader and contributor, eccentricity and elegance and surprise are the singular qualities that make learning an inviting transaction. And they are not qualities we associate with committees.” Yet Wikipedia has eccentricity, elegance, and surprise in abundance, especially in those moments when enthusiasm becomes excess and detail is rendered so finely (and pointlessly) that it becomes beautiful. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w9069dzmzrcf5ad7brq8rr))
- for Wikipedians a sense of humor is at the core of the good-faith collaboration that defines the project [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w92py2abn1bamsa0aagc6y))
- “It's a misconception people work for free,” Wales told the site Hacker Noon in 2018. “They have fun for free.” A 2011 survey of more than 5,000 Wikipedia contributors listed “It's fun” as one of the primary reasons they edited the site. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w94sqsyptf2e6hjbw7ajeq))
- If it is a mistake to keep comparing Wikipedia to *Britannica*, it is another kind of category error to judge Wikipedia against its peers in the internet's top 10. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w96vs78pehhpfnq23z5246))
- Wikipedia ought to serve as a model for many forms of social endeavor online, but its lessons do not translate readily into the commercial sphere. It is a noncommercial enterprise, with no investors or shareholders to appease, no financial imperative to grow or die, and no standing to maintain in the arms race to amass data and attain AI supremacy at all costs. At Jimmy Wales' wedding, one of the maids of honor toasted him as the sole internet mogul who wasn't a billionaire.
  The site has helped its fellow tech behemoths, though, especially with the march of AI. Wikipedia's liberal content licenses and vast information hoard have allowed developers to train neural networks much more quickly, cheaply, and widely than proprietary data sets ever could have. When you ask Apple's Siri or Amazon's Alexa a question, Wikipedia helps provide the answer. When you Google a famous person or place, Wikipedia often informs the “knowledge panel” that appears alongside your search results. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w978a7edk81adqptbh5emn))
- The jokes will still be there. Consider Wikidata's numerical tag for the author Douglas Adams, Q42. In Adams' book *The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy*, a group of hyperintelligent beings build a vast, powerful computer called Deep Thought, which they ask for the “Answer to the Ultimate Question of Life, the Universe, and Everything.” What comes out is the number 42. That wink of self-awareness—at the folly and joy of building something as preposterous and powerful as a world brain—is why, with Wikipedia, you know you are getting the best possible information. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w9dkgb6y8cckcc7acd1v6g))
- perhaps more remarkable than Wikipedia's success is how little its reputation has changed. It was criticized as it rose, and now makes its final ascent to … muted criticism. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w69t1scazds546xrqky7aa))
- Wikipedia has become a community, a library, a constitution, an experiment, a political manifesto—the closest thing there is to an online public square. It is one of the few remaining places that retains the faintly utopian glow of the early World Wide Web. A free encyclopedia encompassing the whole of human knowledge, written almost entirely by unpaid volunteers: Can you believe *that* was the one that worked? [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w6ezx46xy35wkxqhtasev1))
- The site's innovations have always been cultural rather than computational. It was created using existing technology. This remains the single most underestimated and misunderstood aspect of the project: its emotional architecture. Wikipedia is built on the personal interests and idiosyncrasies of its contributors; in fact, without getting gooey, you could even say it is built on love. Editors' passions can drive the site deep into inconsequential territory—exhaustive detailing of dozens of different kinds of embroidery software, lists dedicated to bespectacled baseball players, a brief but moving biographical sketch of Khanzir, the only pig in Afghanistan. No knowledge is truly useless, but at its best, Wikipedia weds this ranging interest to the kind of pertinence where Larry David's “Pretty, pretty good!” is given as an example of rhetorical epizeuxis. At these moments, it can feel like one of the few parts of the internet that is improving. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w6p40xcg5epg18nrw2ey2m))
- crowdsourcing, didn't even exist until 2005, when [two WIRED editors](https://www.wired.com/2006/06/crowds/) coined the word. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w6vfr9z4drh2jjzbw3k79w))
- *Nature* released the first major head-to-head comparison study. It revealed that, for articles on science, at least, the two resources were nearly comparable: *Britannica* averaged three minor mistakes per entry, while Wikipedia averaged four. [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w6whmqvjrw50pk3p8m5n54))
- Noting this half-life in 1974, the critic Peter Prescott wrote that “encyclopedias are like loaves of bread: the sooner used, the better, for they are growing stale before they even reach the shelf.” [[2024-07-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j2w6za7ee12a7nvwzwbx5qsb))

frontmatter.author: Richard Cooke
frontmatter.title: Wikipedia Is the Last Best Place on the Internet
frontmatter.summary: Wikipedia is praised for being a valuable online resource despite past doubts. Its community-driven nature and continuous self-improvement set it apart from traditional encyclopedias. Despite some flaws, Wikipedia remains a remarkable and evolving platform for sharing knowledge.

notes.title: How to Add Text Recognition (OCR) to Notion
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/How to Add Text Recognition (OCR) to Notion.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- [Notion](https://www.notion.so/) is, as for many others, the main part of [my Second Brain setup](https://medium.com/@michielvanbeers/my-second-brain-with-notion-readwise-google-maps-and-much-more-66bc821e9385). Since my Second Brain is based on the Capture, Organize, Distill, Express ([C.O.D.E.](https://www.keepproductive.com/blog/how-to-build-a-second-brain)) concept of Tiago Forte, it is important to me that I can easily capture any interesting piece of content that I come across in my day-to-day life [[2023-10-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hcv63d4kjcwne5pre3rg4xnw))
- I wrapped the whole thing in a Docker container, so it can be quickly distributed, installed and updated on any machine.
  The end result can be found on my Github page [here](https://github.com/MichielvanBeers/notion-auto-ocr), together with a bit more technical write-up on how to set everything up. With this setup, I can now quickly snap a picture of anything important, inspiring or noteworthy that I come across in my day-to-day life. By adding a small tag underneath the picture, the photo will be automatically converted to text and thus increases the chances that it will pop up in the future when I’m searching my notes for inspiration. And [[2023-10-15]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hcv65vas3ha0txrn126dgk8r))

notes.title: How to Bullet Journal in 2026
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/How to Bullet Journal in 2026.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- So now the four core bullets are notes, actions, moods, and events or name. The acronym is a reminder of a core part of our practice observing our experience. [[2026-04-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01knfbv4h5mv3774021kwqk4xb))
- So I created a reflection ritual specifically designed for bujo. I call it TAME. TAME provides a simple and structured reflection framework in these steps. One, tidy your record to make it current and to jog your memory. Two, acknowledge which actions moved you toward or away from your intentions.
  Three, migrate actions that support your intentions and goals with care. Four, enact, literally, to make things real through prioritized action [[2026-04-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01knfbvme8xmz2wtr5hccj7pef))

frontmatter.author: Bullet Journal
frontmatter.title: How to Bullet Journal in 2026
frontmatter.summary: The bullet journal is a personal system that helps you understand and improve your life by writing and reflecting regularly. It uses simple symbols to track notes, actions, moods, and events, helping you become more self-aware and intentional. By setting clear intentions and practicing reflection rituals, you can take meaningful actions to live a better, more connected life.

notes.ref: How to Make Atomic Literature Notes Using the Zettelkasten Method
notes.title: How to Make Atomic Literature Notes Using the Zettelkasten Method
notes.body:
## Highlights
- A fleeting note is a temporary, raw capture of what comes to mind or what you have seen (quotes, excerpts, ideas). [[2022-05-23]] 
- The fleeting note can reference the original material, whether it’s a book, article, video, etc. This allows you to trace back to where you originally captured the information in case you find that your understanding wasn’t correct and you need to review the source material again. [[2022-05-23]] 
- A literature note is where you take individual concepts from your fleeting note and extract the core idea of one of those concepts into a single note. It is written in your own words and that shows you have understood of the idea conveyed. You will end up creating many literature notes from a single fleeting note. [[2022-05-23]]

notes.title: The_Art_of_Unix_Programming
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/The_Art_of_Unix_Programming.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Ifyou are not a programmer, or you are a programmer who has had little contact with the Unix world, this may seem strange. But Unix has a culture; it has a distinctive art of programming; and it carries with it a powerful design philosophy. [[2024-08-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j4k4m41vmtvc2c0x78z0dq4p))
- technology has changed so rapidly, software environments have come and gone so quickly, that technical cultures have been weak and ephemeral. There are, however, exceptions to this exception. A very few software technologies have proved durable enough to evolve strong technical cultures, distinctive arts, and an associated design philosophy transmitted across generations of engineers.
  The Unix culture is one of these. [[2024-08-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j4k4pajctng43bt7z1nmf92r))
- Unix was born in 1969 and has been in continuous production use ever since. That’s several geologic eras by computer-industry standards [[2024-08-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j4k4qq8d19asadqpsqcn9yz6))
- Unix’s durability and adaptability have been nothing short of astonishing. [[2024-08-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j4k4sa9rmzkd80bmfavnfv0x))
- There’s a bedrock ofunchanging basics — languages, system calls, and tool invocations — that one can actually keep using for years, even decades. [[2024-08-05]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01j4k4vac7jf0rknda5nhvtgf2))

frontmatter.title: The_Art_of_Unix_Programming
frontmatter.summary: The content discusses the Unix philosophy, design principles, historical context, criticisms, and its impact on programming culture. It highlights the importance of modularity, simplicity, and flexibility in Unix programming, emphasizing clean interfaces and minimalist approaches. The Unix tradition values user control, open-source collaboration, and evolving design practices.

notes.ref: A Guide on Links vs. Tags in Obsidian
notes.title: A Guide on Links vs. Tags in Obsidian
notes.body:
## Highlights
- What Obsidian calls a link can be defined as: “A connection from one note to another note”.
  In Obsidian, a tag is: “A connection from a note to an idea”. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943898))
- Links auto-refactor by default, and tags do not [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943904))
- When you change the name of a file within Obsidian, all links to that folder will automatically change to be pointing to the right place. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943906))
- Click behavior is very different for tags and links [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943909))
- Clicking on a tag will open a search for all files that contain that tag. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943910))
- Clicking on a link will either:
  Open the file the link is pointing to.
  Create the file the link is pointing to, if it does not already exist. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943918))
- Tags are implicitly counters [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943938))
- Tags can be hierarchical [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943939))
- Links can have aliases [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943941))
- A link can have multiple “names”, in the format of [[A Note Name|An Alias To The Note Name]]. This can be handy, and is not something tags can do. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943943))
- Instances of each tag are automatically counted up by the Obsidian tag pane! This means that ever tag automatically is also a visual counter of how often that tag appears in your vault. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943944))
- Tags can be defined in front-matter [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943952))
- If you want to organize using links, then eventually you are going to encounter the idea of a “higher-order note”.
  You might have heard the term “MoC” used around the Obsidian community. This stands for “map of content”, and they are a kind of “higher-order notes”. A higher-order note is any note that exists to help you navigate to other notes. The naming isn’t 100% agreed on, so don’t let that confuse you. “Indexes”, “MoCs”, “Higher-Order Notes”… They’re all pretty similar. [[2022-06-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://instapaper.com/read/1518194134/19943955))

frontmatter.author: forum.obsidian.md
frontmatter.title: A Guide on Links vs. Tags in Obsidian

notes.ref: The Magical Mystery Four How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why
notes.title: The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/The Magical Mystery Four How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- There are severe limits in how much can be kept in mind at once (~3–5 items) [[2024-02-02]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnccsj1yxmwa323tc2the9wb))
- According to one type of view (e.g., [Kane, Bleckley, Conway, & Engle, 2001](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2864034/#R11); [Vogel, McCollough, & Machizawa, 2005](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2864034/#R15)), low-span individuals remember less because they use up more of their storage capacity holding information that is irrelevant to the assigned task [[2024-02-03]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnsh3txp5qay6t35pbkew4f8))
- Working memory is used in mental tasks, such as language comprehension (for example, retaining ideas from early in a sentence to be combined with ideas later on), problem solving (in arithmetic, carrying a digit from the ones to the tens column while remembering the numbers), and planning (determining the best order in which to visit the bank, library, and grocery). [[2024-01-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnccwm7dnjynvftm3jpa8yk6))
- the participant does not know when a list will end and, when it does, must recall several items from the end of the list [[2024-01-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hncczydewb4am4d88kx5ksz6))
- Though we cannot yet make precise predictions about how well working memory will operate in every possible task, we can measure storage-specific capacity by preventing or controlling processing strategies. [[2024-01-29]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hncdcwjgjphgscn6vncfjw1x))

frontmatter.author: Nelson Cowan
frontmatter.title: The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?
frontmatter.summary: Working memory capacity is limited to holding about 3 to 5 meaningful items at once. This capacity limit is important for tasks that rely on working memory, such as language comprehension, problem solving, and planning. The limit can be observed by preventing processing strategies like rehearsal and grouping. Working memory capacity varies among individuals and can predict differences in intellectual ability. There are ongoing debates about the reasons for this capacity limit, with hypotheses suggesting both weaknesses and strengths in having a limited working memory capacity.

notes.ref: Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking
notes.title: Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Oakley asserts that **if your working memory cannot hold all the information it needs to solve a problem, mental “choking” occurs**, preventing you from solving the problem. The “chunking” process helps to reduce your risk of choking by condensing information, leaving room for your brain to load all the concepts you need at once [[2024-01-16]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hmar6y450bkkfcx204avv1ez))

frontmatter.author: Elizabeth Whitworth
frontmatter.title: Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking
frontmatter.summary: Information chunking is a process in which memories are consolidated into "chunks" of related information in the brain. It is a vital part of the learning process as it helps organize information and improve memory. Working memory and long-term memory are both involved in information chunking. Working memory holds the information actively being processed, while long-term memory stores information for future reference. Choking can occur when working memory cannot hold all the information needed to solve a problem, but chunking helps reduce the risk of choking by condensing information.

notes.ref: There’s No Such Thing as Free Will
notes.title: There’s No Such Thing as Free Will
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/There’s No Such Thing as Free Will.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- In the Christian tradition, this is known as “moral liberty”—the capacity to discern and pursue the good, instead of merely being compelled by appetites and desires. The great Enlightenment philosopher Immanuel Kant reaffirmed this link between freedom and goodness. If we are not free to choose, he argued, then it would make no sense to say we ought to choose the path of righteousness. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvage751khexsfq59tj1sxw))
- the assumption of free will runs through every aspect of American politics, from welfare provision to criminal law [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvagt1qpn615dc4wmqedb1x))
- values are rooted in a basic optimism about life and a faith in free will [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvahc4vzksgkkxzmrrx93d6))
- So what happens if this faith erodes? [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvahkm71wcxxvx49pqmpst3))
- It seems that when people stop believing they are free agents, they stop seeing themselves as blameworthy for their actions [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvapjz0n1frq66f8q1ybmap))
- In fact, belief in free will turned out to be a better predictor of job performance than established measures such as self-professed work ethic. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvaqqhg7az17fmp7t575w4p))
- Further studies by Baumeister and colleagues have linked a diminished belief in free will to stress, unhappiness, and a lesser commitment to relationships. They found that when subjects were induced to believe that “all human actions follow from prior events and ultimately can be understood in terms of the movement of molecules,” those subjects came away with a lower sense of life’s meaningfulness. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvarwnq94prps9d1x2resdj))
- Believing that free will is an illusion has been shown to make people less creative, more likely to conform, less willing to learn from their mistakes, and less grateful toward one another. In every regard, it seems, when we embrace determinism, we indulge our dark side. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvasddwxd97jn4h1z5m0r7b))
- Our heroes would seem less inspiring, he argues, our achievements less noteworthy, and soon we would sink into decadence and despondency. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvaw4zq4wz9r49j2fbjmv05))
- Smilansky advocates a view he calls illusionism—the belief that free will is indeed an illusion, but one that society must defend [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvawn56pvt3kw9h37nqrp72))
- In the language of cause and effect, a belief in free will may not *inspire* us to make the best of ourselves, but it does *stimulate* us to do so. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvay6rc8h6gtqr6geg2kjd2))
- Whereas the evidence from Kathleen Vohs and her colleagues suggests that social problems may arise from seeing *our own* actions as determined by forces beyond our control—weakening our morals, our motivation, and our sense of the meaningfulness of life—Harris thinks that social benefits will result from seeing *other people’s* behavior in the very same light. From that vantage point, the moral implications of determinism look very different, and quite a lot better. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvb4xf8w7er7wb7cjej9rfj))
- the act of deliberation—the wrestling with facts and emotions that we feel is essential to our nature—is nonetheless real. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvb5zetp1r9w59wczdr3rv9))
- People are not moving toward an inevitable destiny; given a different stimulus (like a different idea about free will), they will behave differently and so have different lives. If people better understood these fine distinctions, Harris believes, the consequences of losing faith in free will would be much less negative than Vohs’s and Baumeister’s experiments suggest. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvb7jgxwkt9vf4t6cn7j35g))
- people mostly thought of free will in terms of following their desires, free of coercion [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvbackem2nh5c537051pajs))
- Waller’s account of free will still leads to a very different view of justice and responsibility than most people hold today. No one has caused himself: No one chose his genes or the environment into which he was born. Therefore no one bears ultimate responsibility for who he is and what he does. [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvbb7dxhjjhkvjpeqvpwnkw))
- worst. President Obama—who has both defended “a faith in free will” and argued that we are not the sole architects of our fortune—has had to learn what a fine line this is to tread [[2024-01-10]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hkvbdfag7qc691recpb9w46f))

frontmatter.author: Stephen Cave
frontmatter.title: There’s No Such Thing as Free Will
frontmatter.summary: But we all may be better off believing in it anyway.

notes.ref: Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing
notes.title: Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- A popular phrase warning against complacency has been misattributed to the eighteenth-century Irish philosopher and statesman Edmund Burke.
  “The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing,” the alleged quote reads. Recent examples on social media can be viewed ([here](https://www.facebook.com/quotationsformen/posts/371113047794086)), ([here](https://www.facebook.com/137508053001954/posts/4423100834442633)), ([here](https://www.facebook.com/1719798598279669/posts/2964314490494734)), ([here](https://www.facebook.com/2145164705721385/posts/3022699957967851)), ([here](https://www.instagram.com/p/CSIZQt5tWt7/)), and ([here](https://www.instagram.com/p/CSGIvNPHnKs/)).
  There is no evidence, to date, that Burke said these words, nor has the exact quote been found in his writings. [[2024-01-13]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hm3aawdzcmsyq1e5t4hcpkts))

frontmatter.author: Reuters Fact Check
frontmatter.title: Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing
frontmatter.summary: The popular phrase "The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing" has been misattributed to Edmund Burke. There is no evidence that Burke actually said these words, and the exact quote has not been found in his writings. The misattribution of the quote has persisted, with notable figures such as President John F. Kennedy using it in speeches. Burke did make a similar statement in his writings, but it is not an exact match to the misattributed quote. The origin of the misattribution is likely a paraphrase based on Burke's work.

notes.ref: Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention
notes.title: Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- long-term memory like one's ability to remember the capitals of states or countries the different continents directions from one location to another even one's name all of those things are examples of long-term memory [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf1hdy5x6wgc77hxkhp79ga))
- declarative long-term memories so these are the things that we can declare things like facts about ourselves or the world or others and then there are procedural long-term memories procedural long-term memories as the name suggests are aspects of our memory that allow us to perform certain procedures they are literally action steps that we take to for instance ride a bicycle or drive a car which by the way we might not be conscious of ourselves doing [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf1j6whzzgmpeys4gre1hfw))
- people have damage to their hippocampus of any kind that people have trouble accessing or forming long-term memory sometimes both [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6hhfexbwhbymvwgshxzh9))
- short-term memory [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6jtqmb0hd9w5gscdwyysv))
- represents a short-term memory bank for information that may or may not get passed into long-term memory [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6k3x7j0x11627dx5am2wm))
- short-term memories are the sorts of memories that we maintain for somewhere between a few minutes and potentially a few hours [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6nad7k58bbf6d08h70wqn))
- working memory and how it is different from short and long-term memory is that the formation of short and long-term memories almost always involves neuroplasticity neuroplasticity is the nervous system's ability to change in response to experience [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6rmtfc6fxr1wv5jmgw5b8))
- working memory working memory as far as we know does not involve neuroplasticity [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6w7tnnb6xhjhynsavqq9s))
- working memory is the reflection of a particular neural circuit running an algorithm over and over and over for different types of information but the information isn't stored it is actually intentionally discarded [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6y0fexn3n6k4cqgmn28gd))
- working memory is basically the taking in of information that's critical for you to sequence your actions over a short period of time and then forget [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf6z0ee3sx9vh4nc8ndmtsp))
- you simply want to be able to carry out that sequence and then discard that information about the sequence [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf7179b5an3ssggdkvnhdgn))
- working memory and attention collaborate literally at a neural circuit level and at a neurochemical level in order to allow you to move through your day in an Adaptive functional way [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf726q9z5e2gabcbm3gq4gz))
- working memory works so in some sense the working memory task is a bit unusual in that it's a test of yes memory in the very very short term but also a test of your ability to forget to discard information that's not critical and that gets us back to the original definition of working memory which is our ability to attend to specific small batches of information remember it for just as long as we think we need to and then to discard that information and by the way if you want to know what those first five letters were they were j k z p i [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnf7p191ejmx5sbqrm61ht2r))
- I want to remind everybody that working memory capacity follows a normal distribution so some of you will be able to remember the final word of perhaps five or even six of those sentences although I must say that is exceedingly rare some of you are going to be able to remember three to four of the final words of those sentences and that's more typical that actually represents the average or the mean as we call it and
  then fewer people although still many of you will only be able to remember one or two of the final words [[2024-01-31]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnfef6vzk28fxb7rxazx8ekn))

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention
frontmatter.summary: Working memory is a type of memory that allows us to hold small amounts of information in our mind for short periods of time. It is closely related to attention and can be improved through various tools and techniques. Working memory is different from long-term memory, which involves the storage of facts and procedures, and short-term memory, which acts as a temporary memory bank. The formation of short and long-term memories involves neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to change and adapt in response to experience. Different types of neuroplasticity, such as long-term potentiation and long-term depression, play a role in memory formation.

frontmatter.author: Mark Manson
frontmatter.title: How to Quiet Your Ego
frontmatter.summary: The ego helps us make sense of the world and protects our sense of self. A healthy ego is quiet, flexible, and realistic, while an unhealthy ego is rigid and fragile. Practices like meditation and journaling can help quiet the ego and strengthen our true self.

You're   like, "Hey, I don't do this or this isn't my thing  or that's not a priority for me." Yeah. And I   think, you know, in the in the values episode, we  talked about how values, and you've mentioned this   too, here, values are acted out. They're they're  the way you behave.   or uncared for, when my partner doesn't clean up  after themselves, we set rules and expectations of   who's in charge of cleaning the kitchen on which  days or after which meals. [[2026-01-07]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kedqgwe4fnnpt4mw077w31fb))
- The importance thing about uh boundaries is that  they are stated assertively but not aggressively.   So there's a very subtle difference. Yeah, the way  I would describe this is like, you know, stating   something passively is stating it in a way that  you take no ownership or autonomy over it. So,   it' be something like, you know, Drew, it'd be  really nice if like when we recorded podcasts,  
  uh, the AC was turned on. You know, it just  be nice if I wasn't hot all the time. Like,   it's such a passive. It's almost like it's making  it your problem. That'd be nice. Yeah. Exactly.   It's like I'm taking zero ownership of it.  And so the the effect of that is that I am   subcommunicating that it's actually your problem  and your fault that I'm hot, which is not the way   you want to go about this. That's not effective at  all. Whereas I I would say doing it aggressively  
  is demanding something and also blaming, taking no  ownership over it. So, if I was to state state it   aggressively, it would be something like, uh, you  know, Drew, every time we record these podcasts,   I'm hot as [ __ ] and it'd be great if you  got your [ __ ] together and turn on the AC   once in a while. Like, it's disrespectful,  it's condescending, it's demeaning, and   I'm taking no ownership of it whatsoever. So, an  assertive way of saying it would be, "Hey, Drew,  
  every time we're shooting these podcasts, I get  really hot. How about we agree to uh turn on the   AC when we take breaks in between shoots? How does  that sound to you? Right? It's like I'm owning it.   I'm suggesting a solution. I'm telling you exactly  I feel this way in this situation. How about we do   this? I'm bringing you in as part of the boundary,  too. Right. It's a collaboration where and you and   it opens it it makes it clear that you don't  have to agree to it. You could be like, "Well,  
  I'm cold all the time." Yeah. And then that Okay.  So now we change the conversation. We'll get to   negotiating a little bit later. Right. So that's  an example of I guess passive doing it passively.   I would even call it passive aggressiveness.  Aggressiveness. You want assertiveness. You   want to own it. Be like, "Hey, this is my feeling.  This is what I don't like and this is how I would   like to see it changed. How do you feel about  that?" Right? So that's step number two. [[2026-01-06]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kebhe2h999s9bvrp9czjjvsq))

notes.ref: Exposing the Biggest Fraud in Self-Help History
notes.title: Exposing the Biggest Fraud in Self-Help History
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Exposing the Biggest Fraud in Self-Help History.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- My good friend Derek Civers, the author and entrepreneur.
  Recently wrote a book about the importance of adopting beliefs that are what he calls useful, not true. His argument is that most things are hard to know for sure. So, you might as well defer to believing whatever is most helpful for you and others. And that may make the scientists in the room bristle and shake their fists and demand that every capital T true thing be empirically verified. But Derek points out that we do this all the time.
  Art is useful, not true. Religion is useful, not true. Political beliefs and economic forecasts and the fact that a guy kicked the ball into a net more than another guy makes him a winner. These are all useful, not true beliefs. And we lean on them all the time. This framework, judging beliefs by their usefulness rather than their correspondence to some abstract reality, it offers a new way to think about Napoleon Hill and self-help in general. Judge ideas by the actions they inspire, not by the literal. [[2026-02-08]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgztzt8paa6nj9789c3ayqnx))
- Now on The book's entire authority rests on the lie. Think and Grow Rich isn't pitched as Napoleon Hill's personal philosophy based on his experiences. It's pitched as distilled wisdom from 500 of history's most successful men delivered by Andrew Carnegie himself. Carnegie's name is invoked in the book constantly. The whole premise is that Hill is just a conduit for Andrew Carnegie's wisdom and the wisdom of other titans. So if you remove the origin story, the book loses its weight. It becomes one guy's opinion about success, which might still be valuable, but it's a very, very different product. And this is where self-help gets ethically complicated. Where is that line of what's useful and not useful?
  Not even for yourself, but for the audience. When does a lie help people and when does it hurt people? Lying about Andrew Carnegie didn't hurt Andrew Carnegie, but it helped millions of people. So, you could say, 'Who cares? That's all that matters.' And you.
  See this argument comes up all the time in all sorts of places—art, film, music. I mean, does knowing Michael Jackson's personal life make Billy Gene a worse song? Does knowing Picasso's treatment of women make his paintings less of a masterpiece? Probably not. But here's the difference. Michael Jackson's personal life doesn't affect whether Billy Gene has a good beat or not. The song exists independently of his biography. Picasso's cruelty doesn't change the brush strokes on the canvas.
  But Napoleon Hill's fabrications are the [ __ ] product. The book's authority comes from the lie. The creator and the credibility can't be separated because the claimed credibility is the entire basis for the advice in the first place. So, it's not separate art from the artist. It's the artist's fake credentials that are the art. So, is it worth it? Is the benefit of the ideas, the usefulness, the help to millions and millions of people, worth it?
  Is it worth the harm of the fabrications and the dishonesty? [[2026-02-08]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01kgzv1d825fdwegrq17v7mqm2))

frontmatter.author: Mark Manson
frontmatter.title: Exposing the Biggest Fraud in Self-Help History
frontmatter.summary: Napoleon Hill wrote the famous self-help book *Think and Grow Rich*, claiming it was based on wisdom from wealthy leaders like Andrew Carnegie. However, much of Hill's story and claims were false, as he was a con artist with a troubled past. Despite this, the book's ideas still inspire people because they encourage useful actions, even if not all are true.

notes.ref: Working Memory
notes.title: Working Memory
notes.body:
## Full Document
[[Full Document Contents/Articles/Working Memory.md|See full document content →]]

## Highlights
- Representations in working memory are a subset of representations in long-term memory. Working memory is organized into two embedded levels. The first consists of long-term memory representations that are activated. There can be many of these—there is theoretically no limit to the activation of representations in long-term memory. The second level is called the focus of attention. The focus is regarded as having a limited capacity and holds up to four of the activated representations.[[17](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-Cowan_1995-17) [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hndra45b99gjh1sneey3kwq7))
- Working memory is widely acknowledged as having limited capacity [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnds2e2khs1w74zbncsf8t04))
- Several other factors affect a person's measured span, and therefore it is difficult to pin down the capacity of short-term or working memory to a number of chunks. Nonetheless, Cowan proposed that working memory has a capacity of about four chunks in young adults (and fewer in children and old adults).[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-23) [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hndv90q1ymdc31d7qqy13qm4))
- Daneman and Carpenter invented the first version of this kind of task, the "[reading span](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reading_span)", in 1980.[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-29) Subjects read a number of sentences (usually between two and six) and tried to remember the last word of each sentence [[2024-01-30]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnebcg7n2dm0ammrh666ynzh))
- The one-element focus is embedded in the four-element focus and serves to select a single chunk for processing. For example, four digits can be held in mind at the same time in Cowan's "focus of attention". When the individual wishes to perform a process on each of these digits—for example, adding the number two to each digit—separate processing is required for each digit since most individuals cannot perform several mathematical processes in parallel.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-18) Oberauer's attentional component selects one of the digits for processing and then shifts the attentional focus to the next digit, continuing until all digits have been processed.[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-19) [[2024-01-31]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnchzmgyah1b4ncdnppenbxp))
- Measures of working-memory capacity are strongly related to performance in other complex cognitive tasks, such as reading comprehension, problem solving, and with measures of [intelligence quotient](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intelligence_quotient).[[36]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-36)
  Some researchers have argued[[37]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Working_memory#cite_note-37) that working-memory capacity reflects the efficiency of executive functions, most notably the ability to maintain multiple task-relevant representations in the face of distracting irrelevant information; and that such tasks seem to reflect individual differences in the ability to focus and maintain attention, particularly when other events are serving to capture attention [[2024-02-04]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnrhw4m92ec61vjhf9qg9w33))
- Other researchers have argued that the capacity of working memory is better characterized as the ability to mentally form relations between elements, or to grasp relations in given information. [[2024-02-04]]  ([View Highlight](https://read.readwise.io/read/01hnrhwnn413cha6xm2v9s9cvf))

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Working Memory
frontmatter.summary: Working memory is a complex cognitive process that involves the temporary storage and manipulation of information. It is essential for tasks such as problem-solving, decision-making, and learning. Research has shown that working memory is influenced by genetic factors and can vary between individuals. Additionally, working memory capacity tends to decline with age and can be affected by neurological conditions such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. Understanding the mechanisms and limitations of working memory is crucial for developing interventions and treatments for cognitive impairments.

notes.ref: Getting Things Done
notes.title: Getting Things Done
notes.body:
## Highlights
- There is a way to get a grip on it all, stay relaxed, and get meaningful things done with minimal effort, across the whole spectrum of your life and work. You can experience what the martial artists call a “mind like water” and top athletes refer to as the “zone,” within the complex world in which you’re engaged. In fact, you have probably already been in this state from time to time. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 547](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=547))
- World-class rower Craig Lambert has described how it feels in Mind Over Water (Houghton Mifflin, 1998): [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 555](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=555))
- In karate there is an image that’s used to define the position of perfect readiness: “mind like water.” Imagine throwing a pebble into a still pond. How does the water respond? The answer is, totally appropriately to the force and mass of the input; then it returns to calm. It doesn’t overreact or underreact. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 563](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=563))
- Water is what it is, and does what it does. It can overwhelm, but it’s not overwhelmed. It can be still, but it is not impatient. It can be forced to change course, but it is not frustrated. Get it? [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 568](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=568))
- Most people have major weaknesses in their (1) capture process. Most of their commitments to do something are still in their head. The number of coulds, shoulds, might-want-tos, and ought-tos they generate in their minds are way out beyond what they have recorded anywhere else. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 849](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=849))
- In order for your mind to let go of the lower-level task of trying to hang on to everything, you have to know that you have truly captured everything that might represent something you have to do or at least decide about, and that at some point in the near future you will process and review all of it. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 879](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=879))
- The way I look at it, the calendar should be sacred territory. If you write something [[2024-06-26]]  ([Location 1119](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=1119))
- as you’re processing your input: “Is this really necessary or useful for me to keep, or can I trust that I can access it from the Internet or other sources if I need it?” [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2653](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2653))
- There will probably be things in your in-tray about which you will say to yourself, “There’s nothing to do on this now, but there might be later.” [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2655](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2655))
- Put a reminder of them on your calendar or in a tickler file. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2665](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2665))
- This is perhaps the most fundamental practice of this methodology. If there’s something that needs to be done about the item in “in,” then you need to decide what, exactly, that next action is. “Next action,” again, means the next physical, visible activity that would be required to move the situation toward closure. This is both easier and more difficult than it sounds. The next action should be easy to figure out, but there are often some quick analyses and several planning steps that haven’t occurred yet in your mind, and these have to happen before you can determine precisely what has to happen to complete the item, even if it’s a fairly simple one. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2700](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2700))
- The Action Step Needs to Be the Absolute Next Physical Thing to Do Until you know what the next physical action is, there’s still more thinking required before anything can happen—before you’re appropriately engaged. Remember that these are physical, visible activities. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2719](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2719))
- You have three options once you decide what the next action really is: Do it (if the action takes less than two minutes). Delegate it (if you’re not the most appropriate person to do the action). Defer it into your organization system as an option for work to do later. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2737](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2737))
- One vice president of a large software company told me that it gave him an additional hour a day of quality discretionary time! He was one of those three-hundred-e-mail-a-day high-tech executives, highly focused for most of the workday on three key initiatives. Many of those e-mails were from people who reported to him—and they needed his eyes on something, his comments and OKs, in order to move forward. But because they were not on a topic in his rifle sights, he would just stage the e-mails in “in,” to get to “later.” After several thousand of them piled up, he would have to go in to work and spend whole weekends trying to catch up. That would have been OK if he were twenty-six, when everything’s an adrenaline rush anyway, but he was in his thirties and had young kids. Working all weekend was no longer acceptable behavior. When I coached him he went through all eight-hundred-plus e-mails he currently had in “in.” It turned out that a lot could be dumped, quite a few needed to be filed as reference, and many others required less-than-two-minute replies that he whipped through. I checked with him a year later, and he was still current! [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2748](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2748))
- a significant category to manage is Waiting For. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2806](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2806))
- It’s important that you record the date on everything that you hand off to others. This, of all the categories in your personal system, is the most crucial one to keep tabs on. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2813](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2813))
- I define a project as any outcome you’re committed to achieving that will take more than one action step to complete. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2830](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2830))
- A Projects list Project support material Calendar actions and information Next Actions lists A Waiting For list Reference material A Someday/Maybe list [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2868](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2868))
- It’s critical that all of these categories be kept pristinely distinct from one another. They each represent a discrete type of agreement we make with ourselves, to be reminded of at a specific time and in a specific way, and if they lose their edges and begin to blend, much of the value of organizing will be lost. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2871](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2871))
- You need to trust your calendar as sacred territory, reflecting the exact hard edges of your day’s commitments, which should be noticeable at a glance while you’re on the run. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2924](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2924))
- Over many years I have discovered that the best way to be reminded of an “as soon as I can” action is by the particular context required for that action—that is, either the tool or the location or the situation needed to complete it. For instance, if the action requires a computer, it should go on an At Computer list. If your action demands that you be out and moving around in the world (such as stopping by the bank or going to the hardware store), the Errands list would be the appropriate place to track it. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2929](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2929))
- Calls At Computer Errands At Office (miscellaneous) At Home Anywhere Agendas (for people and meetings) Read/Review [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 2947](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=2947))
- I recommend that separate lists be kept for bosses, partners, assistants, and children. You should also keep the same kind of list for your attorney, financial adviser, accountant, and/or computer consultant, as well as for anyone else with whom you might have more than one thing to go over the next time you talk on the phone or in person. [[2023-10-17]]  ([Location 3007](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=3007))
- Remember, you can’t do a project; you can only do the action steps it requires. [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 3156](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=3156))
- The real value of the Projects list lies in the complete review it can provide (at least once a week), ensuring that you have action steps defined for all of your projects and that nothing is slipping through the cracks. A quick glance at this list from time to time will enhance your underlying sense of control. [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 3158](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=3158))
- may be challenging to realize that “look into getting a dog for our kids” and “find a good tailor” are projects! [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 3164](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=3164))
- It is impossible to be truly relaxed and in your productive state when things you’ve told yourself you need to handle continue to pull at your mind—whether they be little or big. [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 3170](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=3170))
- Projects often don’t show up in nice, neat packages. They start as what seems a simple situation, communication, or activity, but they slowly morph into something bigger than you expected. [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 3177](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B00KWG9M2E&location=3177))

frontmatter.author: David Allen
frontmatter.title: Getting Things Done

notes.ref: Thomas Jefferson
notes.title: Thomas Jefferson
notes.body:
## Highlights
- “When I recollect the various sorts of bad company with which I associated from time to time, I am astonished I did not turn off with some of them, and become as worthless to society as they were.... But I had the good fortune to become acquainted very early with some characters of very high standing, and to feel the incessant wish that I could ever become what they were. Under temptations and difficulties, I would ask myself what would Dr. Small, Mr. Wythe, Peyton Randolph do in this situation? What course in it will as[pg 14] sure me their approbation? I am certain that this mode of deciding on my conduct tended more to correctness than any reasoning powers that I possesed.” [[2012-01-18]]  ([Location 140](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B004TQ0BNC&location=140))
- This passage throws a light upon Jefferson’s character. It does not seem to occur to him that a young man might require some stronger motive to keep his passions in check than could be furnished either by the wish to imitate a good example or by his “reasoning powers.” To Jefferson’s well-regulated mind the desire for approbation was a sufficient motive. He was particularly sensitive, perhaps morbidly so, to disapprobation. [[2012-01-18]]  ([Location 145](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B004TQ0BNC&location=145))
- Virginia society just before the Revolution perfectly illustrated Buckle’s remark about leisure: “Without leisure, science is impos[pg 21] sible; and when leisure has been won, most of the class possessing it will waste it in the pursuit of pleasure, and a few will employ it in the pursuit of knowledge.” [[2012-01-18]]  ([Location 195](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B004TQ0BNC&location=195))
- him;—it was as a writer, not as a speaker, that he won fame, first in the Virginia Assembly, and afterward in the Continental Congress. [[2012-01-18]]  ([Location 262](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B004TQ0BNC&location=262))
- general,—“soothing always the feelings of his adversaries by civilities and softnesses of expression.” [[2012-01-18]]  ([Location 440](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B004TQ0BNC&location=440))
- Jefferson was for abolishing all connection between church and state, [[2012-01-18]]  ([Location 443](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B004TQ0BNC&location=443))
