notes.ref: SGGS
notes.title: SGGS
notes.body:
## Highlights
- ਹਮ ਕੀਆ ਹਮ ਕਰਹਗੇ ਹਮ ਮੂਰਖ ਗਾਵਾਰ ॥ "I have done this, and I will do that”-I am an idiotic fool for saying this!
  ਕਰਣੈ ਵਾਲਾ ਵਿਸਰਿਆ ਦੂਜੈ ਭਾਇ ਪਿਆਰੁ ॥ I have forgotten the Doer of all; I am caught in the love of duality.
  ਮਾਇਆ ਜੇਵਡੁ ਦੁਖੁ ਨਹੀ ਸਭਿ ਭਵਿ ਥਕੇ ਸੰਸਾਰੁ ॥ There is no pain as great as the pain of Maya; it drives people to wander all around the world, until they become exhausted.
  ਗੁਰਮਤੀ ਸੁਖੁ ਪਾਈਐ ਸਚੁ ਨਾਮੁ ਉਰ ਧਾਰਿ ॥੩॥ Through the Guru's Teachings, peace is found, with the True Name enshrined in the heart. 11311 [[2022-06-09]]  (Page 39)
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- ਮੈ ਮਨਿ ਤੇਰੀ ਟੇਕ ਮੇਰੇ ਪਿਆਰੇ ਮੈ ਮਨਿ ਤੇਰੀ ਟੇਕ ॥ You are the Support of my mind, O my Beloved, You are the Support of my mind.
  ਅਵਰ ਸਿਆਣਪਾ ਬਿਰਥੀਆ ਪਿਆਰੇ ਰਾਖਨ ਕਉ ਤੁਮ ਏਕ ॥੧॥ ਰਹਾਉ ॥ All other clever tricks are useless, O Beloved; You alone are my Protector. || 1 | | Pause | | [[2022-06-09]]  (Page 802)
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
    - Note: SGGS Guru Arjun Dev Ji
- ਭਰਮਿ ਭੁਲਾਈ ਸਭੁ ਜਗੁ ਫਿਰੀ ਫਾਵੀ ਹੋਈ ਭਾਲਿ ॥ Deluded by doubt, I wandered over the whole world. Searching, I became frustrated.
  ਸੋ ਸਹੁ ਸਾਂਤਿ ਨ ਦੇਵਈ ਕਿਆ ਚਲੈ ਤਿਸੁ ਨਾਲਿ ॥ My Husband Lord has not blessed me with peace and tranquility; what will work with Him?
  ਗੁਰ ਪਰਸਾਦੀ ਹਰਿ ਧਿਆਈਐ ਅੰਤਰਿ ਰਖੀਐ ਉਰ ਧਾਰਿ ॥ By Guru's Grace, I meditate on the Lord; I enshrine Him deep within my heart.
  ਨਾਨਕ ਘਰਿ ਬੈਠਿਆ ਸਹੁ ਪਾਇਆ ਜਾ ਕਿਰਪਾ ਕੀਤੀ ਕਰਤਾਰਿ ॥੧॥ O Nanak, seated in her own home, she finds her Husband Lord, when the Creator Lord grants His Grace. ||1|| ਮਃ ੩ ॥ [[2022-06-13]] 
- ਸਦਾ ਅਨੰਦੁ ਨ ਹੋਵੀ ਸੋਗੁ ॥ I am in bliss forever; I have no sorrow.
  ਕਬਹੂ ਨ ਬਿਆਪੈ ਹਉਮੈ ਰੋਗੁ ॥੧॥ The disease of ego never afflicts me. ||1|| [[2022-07-02]] 
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- ਆਸਾ ਮਨਸਾ ਸਭ ਤੇਰੀ ਮੇਰੇ ਸੁਆਮੀ ਜੈਸੀ ਕਰਾਵਹਿ ਤੈਸੀ ਕੋ ਆਸ ਕਰਾਈ ॥ ਤੂ ਆਸ Hope and desire are all Yours, O my Lord and Master. As You inspire hope, so are the hopes held.
  ਕਿਛੁ ਕਿਸੀ ਕੈ ਹਥਿ ਨਾਹੀ ਮੇਰੇ ਸੁਆਮੀ ਐਸੀ ਮੇਰੈ ਸਤਿਗੁਰਿ ਬੂਝ ਬੁਝਾਈ ॥ Nothing is in the hands of anyone, O my Lord and Master; such is the understanding the True Guru has given me to understand.
  ਜਨ ਨਾਨਕ ਕੀ ਆਸ ਤੂ ਜਾਣਹਿ ਹਰਿ ਦਰਸਨੁ ਦੇਖਿ ਹਰਿ ਦਰਸਨਿ ਤ੍ਰਿਪਤਾਈ ॥੪॥੧॥ You alone know the hope of servant Nanak, O Lord; gazing upon the Blessed Vision of the Lord's Darshan, he is satisfied. [[2022-08-07]] 
- ਤੇਰਾ ਜਨੁ ਰਾਮ ਨਾਮ ਰੰਗਿ ਜਾਗਾ ॥ Your humble servant awakes in the Love of the Lord's Name.
  ਆਲਸੁ ਛੀਜਿ ਗਇਆ ਸਭੁ ਤਨ ਤੇ ਪ੍ਰੀਤਮ ਸਿਉ ਮਨੁ ਲਾਗਾ ॥ ਰਹਾਉ ॥ All laziness has departed from his body, and his mind is attached to the Beloved Lord. || Pause | | [[2022-08-19]] 
- ਹਰਿ ਅੰਮ੍ਰਿਤ ਭਿੰਨੇ ਲੋਇਣਾ ਮਨੁ ਪ੍ਰੇਮਿ ਰਤੰਨਾ ਰਾਮ ਰਾਜੇ ॥ My eyes are wet with the Nectar of the Lord, and my mind is imbued with His Love, O Lord King.
  h ਮਨੁ ਰਾਮਿ ਕਸਵਟੀ ਲਾਇਆ ਕੰਚਨੁ ਸੋਵਿੰਨਾ ॥ The Lord applied His touch-stone to my mind, and found it one hundred per cent gold.
  ਗੁਰਮੁਖਿ ਰੰਗਿ ਚਲੂਲਿਆ ਮੇਰਾ ਮਨੁ ਤਨੋ ਭਿੰਨਾ ॥ As Gurmukh, I am dyed in the deep red of the poppy, and my mind and body are drenched with His Love.
  ਜਨੁ ਨਾਨਕੁ ਮੁਸਕਿ ਝਕੋਲਿਆ ਸਭੁ ਜਨਮੁ ਧਨੁ ਧੰਨਾ ॥੧॥ Servant Nanak is drenched with His Fragrance; blessed, blessed is his entire life. ||1|| [[2022-09-05]] 
- ਭਗਤਾ ਕੀ ਚਾਲ ਨਿਰਾਲੀ ॥ The lifestyle of the devotees is unique and distinct.
  ਚਾਲਾ ਨਿਰਾਲੀ ਭਗਤਾਹ ਕੇਰੀ ਬਿਖਮ ਮਾਰਗਿ ਚਲਣਾ ॥ The devotees' lifestyle is unique and distinct; they follow the most difficult path.
  ਲਬੁ ਲੋਭੁ ਅਹੰਕਾਰੁ ਤਜਿ ਤ੍ਰਿਸਨਾ ਬਹੁਤੁ ਨਾਹੀ ਬੋਲਣਾ ॥ They renounce greed, avarice, egotism and desire; they do not talk too much.
  ਖੰਨਿਅਹੁ ਤਿਖੀ ਵਾਲਹੁ ਨਿਕੀ ਏਤੁ ਮਾਰਗਿ ਜਾਣਾ ॥ The path they take is sharper than a two-edged sword, and finer than a hair.
  ਗੁਰਪਰਸਾਦੀ ਜਿਨ੍ਹੀ ਆਪੁ ਤਜਿਆ ਹਰਿ ਵਾਸਨਾ ਸਮਾਣੀ ॥ By Guru's Grace, they shed their selfishness and conceit; their hopes are merged in the Lord.
  ਕਹੈ ਨਾਨਕੁ ਚਾਲ ਭਗਤਾ ਜੁਗਹੁ ਜੁਗੁ ਨਿਰਾਲੀ ॥੧੪॥ Says Nanak, the lifestyle of the devotees, in each and every age, is unique and distinct. ||14|| [[2022-10-05]]

notes.ref: The World of Ice & Fire
notes.title: The World of Ice & Fire

notes.ref: The Big Picture
notes.title: The Big Picture
notes.body:
## Highlights
- The usual argument against free will is straightforward: We are made of atoms, and those atoms follow the patterns we refer to as the laws of physics. These laws serve to completely describe the evolution of a system, without any influences from outside the atomic description. If information is conserved through time, the entire future of the universe is already written, even if we don’t know it yet. Quantum mechanics predicts our future in terms of probabilities rather than certainties, but those probabilities themselves are absolutely fixed by the state of the universe right now. A quantum version of Laplace’s Demon could say with confidence what the probability of every future history will be, and no amount of human volition would be able to change it. There is no room for human choice, so there is no such thing as free will. We are just material objects who obey the laws of nature. [[2024-04-16]]  ([Location 5731](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B014EOUMZA&location=5731))
- The answer comes down to the arrow of time. In chapter 8 we talked about how we have epistemic access to the past—memories—that we don’t have when it comes to the future. That’s because there is a special boundary condition, the Past Hypothesis, according to which entropy was very low near the Big Bang. That’s a powerful bit of information about the past, which enables us to pin it down in a way that we can’t pin down the future. This temporal asymmetry arises only because of the distribution of matter in the universe on macroscopic scales; there is no analogue of it in the Core Theory itself. There is a crucial role played by the leverage that features of our current state exert over our knowledge of events in the past or future. When a feature of our current state implies (given the Past Hypothesis, and all else being equal) something about the past, that’s a memory; when a feature of our current state implies something about the future, that’s a cause of some future effect. The small differences in a person’s brain state that correlate with different bodily actions typically have negligible correlations with the past state of the universe, but they can be correlated with substantially different future evolutions. That’s why our best human-sized conception of the world treats the past and future so differently. We remember the past, and our choices affect the future. [[2024-04-16]]  ([Location 5761](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B014EOUMZA&location=5761))

notes.ref: How to Take Smart Notes
notes.title: How to Take Smart Notes
notes.body:
## Highlights
- They seem to forget that the process of writing starts much, much earlier than that blank screen and that the actual writing down of the argument is the smallest part of its development. This book aims to fill this gap by showing you how to efficiently turn your thoughts and discoveries into convincing written pieces and build up a treasure of smart and interconnected notes along the way. You can use this pool of notes not only to make writing easier and more fun for yourself, but also to learn for the long run and generate new ideas. But most of all, you can write every day in a way that brings your projects forward. [[2019-03-11]]  ([Location 103](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B06WVYW33Y&location=103))
    - Note: Interesting

notes.ref: Building a Second Brain
notes.title: Building a Second Brain
notes.body:
## Highlights
- Instantaneous access to the world’s knowledge through the Internet was supposed to educate and inform us, but instead it has created a society-wide poverty of attention.I [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 234](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=234))
- For modern, professional notetaking, a note is a “knowledge building block”—a discrete unit of information interpreted through your unique perspective and stored outside your head. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 311](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=311))
- A note could include a single quote from a film that really struck you, all the way to thousands of words you saved from an in-depth book. The length and format don’t matter—if a piece of content has been interpreted through your lens, curated according to your taste, translated into your own words, or drawn from your life experience, and stored in a secure place, then it qualifies as a note. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 314](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=314))
- There are four essential capabilities that we can rely on a Second Brain to perform for us: Making our ideas concrete. Revealing new associations between ideas. Incubating our ideas over time. Sharpening our unique perspectives. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 443](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=443))
- Quotes from a philosophy book written in ancient times might sit next to the latest clever tweet. Screenshots from an interesting YouTube video can live right by scenes from classic movies. An audio memo might be saved alongside project plans, a link to a helpful website, and a PDF with the latest research findings. All [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 470](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=470))
- This tendency is known as recency bias.4 We tend to favor the ideas, solutions, and influences that occurred to us most recently, regardless of whether they are the best ones. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 481](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=481))
- Our careers and businesses depend more than ever on our ability to advance a particular point of view and persuade others to adopt it as well. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 495](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=495))
- Action-oriented: Unlike a library or research database, personal notes don’t need to be comprehensive or precise. They are designed to help you quickly capture stray thoughts so you can remain focused on the task at hand. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 520](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=520))
- try. We need to adopt the perspective of a curator, stepping back from the raging river and starting to make intentional decisions about what information we want to fill our minds. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 580](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=580))
- The solution is to keep only what resonates in a trusted place that you control, and to leave the rest aside. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 587](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=587))
- By training ourselves to notice when something resonates with us, we can improve not only our ability to take better notes, but also our understanding of ourselves and what makes us tick. It is a way of turning up the volume on our intuition so we can hear the wisdom it offers us. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 591](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=591))
- The best way to organize your notes is to organize for action, according to the active projects you are working on right now. Consider new information in terms of its utility, asking, “How is this going to help me move forward one of my current projects?” [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 603](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=603))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- Organizing for action gives you a sense of tremendous clarity, because you know that everything you’re keeping actually has a purpose. You know that it aligns with your goals and priorities. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 607](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=607))
- What is the point of knowledge if it doesn’t help anyone or produce anything? [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 630](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=630))
- This is why I recommend you shift as much of your time and effort as possible from consuming to creating.V [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 640](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=640))
- All these actions—evaluate, share, teach, record, post, and lobbyVI—are synonyms for the act of expression. They all draw on outside sources for raw material, they all involve a practical process of refinement over time, and they all end up making an impact on someone or something that matters to you. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 648](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=648))
- “I disappear into my phone because my phone is where I keep my notes and my phone is where I’m editing.”2 In her notes she can write down (and reread, edit, and riff off) any snippet of lyrics or melodic hook that flickers through her mind. She can take her notes everywhere, access them from anywhere, and send them within seconds to a wide network of producers and collaborators using the same device. Any feedback they send back can go right into her notes as well. [[2023-12-14]]  ([Location 706](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=706))
- Swift says, “I’ll be going about my daily life and I’ll think, ‘Wow, so we only have two real options in relationships—it’s going to be forever or it’s going to go down in flames,’ so I’ll jot that down in my notes… I’ll come up with a line that I think is clever like ‘Darling I’m a nightmare dressed like a daydream’ and I just pick them and put them where they fit and construct the bridge out of more lines that come up within the last couple of years… ‘Blank Space’ was the culmination of all my best ones one after the other.” [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 711](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=711))
- Knowledge capture is about mining the richness of the reading you’re already doing and the life you’re already living. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 753](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=753))
- The meaning of a thought, insight, or memory often isn’t immediately clear. We need to write them down, revisit them, and view them from a different perspective in order to digest what they mean to us. It is exceedingly difficult to do that within the confines of our heads. We need an external medium in which to see our ideas from another vantage point, and writing things down is the most effective and convenient one ever invented. [[2023-12-14]]  ([Location 772](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=772))
- In other words, Feynman’s approach was to maintain a list of a dozen open questions. When a new scientific finding came out, he would test it against each of his questions to see if it shed any new light on the problem. This cross-disciplinary approach allowed him to make connections across seemingly unrelated subjects, while continuing to follow his sense of curiosity. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 808](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=808))
- After working the problem out on paper, Feynman discovered a 2-to-1 ratio between the plate’s wobble and spin, a neat relationship that suggested a deeper underlying principle at work. When a fellow physicist and mentor asked what the use of such an insight was, Feynman responded: “It doesn’t have any importance… I don’t care whether a thing has importance. Isn’t it fun?” [[2023-12-25]]  ([Location 817](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=817))
- The key to this exercise is to make them open-ended questions that don’t necessarily have a single answer. To find questions that invoke a state of wonder and curiosity about the amazing world we live in. The power of your favorite problems is that they tend to stay fairly consistent over time. The exact framing of each question may change, but even as we move between projects, jobs, relationships, and careers, our favorite problems tend to follow us across the years. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 838](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=838))
- The more economical you can be with the material you capture in the first place, the less time and effort your future self will have to spend organizing, distilling, and expressing it.II [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 880](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=880))
- I often save screenshots of text messages sent between my family and friends. The small moments of warmth and humor that take place in these threads are precious to me, since I can’t always be with them in person. It takes mere moments, and I love knowing that I’ll forever have memories from my conversations with the people closest to me. [[2023-12-14]]  ([Location 901](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=901))
- Claude Shannon, whose discoveries paved the way for modern technology, had a simple definition for “information”: that which surprises you. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 907](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=907))
- As you consume a piece of content, listen for an internal feeling of being moved or surprised by the idea you’re taking in. This special feeling of “resonance”—like an echo in your soul—is your intuition telling you that something is literally “noteworthy.” You don’t need to figure out exactly why it resonates. Just look for the signs: your eyes might widen slightly, your heart may skip a beat, your throat may go slightly dry, and your sense of time might subtly slow down as the world around you [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 926](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=926))
- Participants in a famous study were given four biased decks of cards—some that would win them money, and some that would cause them to lose. When they started the game, they didn’t know that the decks were biased. As they played the game, though, people’s bodies started showing signs of physical “stress” when their conscious minds were about to use a money-losing deck. The stress was an automatic response that occurred because the intuitive mind realized something was wrong—long before the conscious mind realized anything was amiss. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 935](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=935))
- I can’t think of anything more important for your creative life—and your life in general—than learning to listen to the voice of intuition inside. It is the source of your imagination, your confidence, and your spontaneity. You can intentionally train yourself to hear that voice of intuition every day by taking note of what it tells you. [[2022-06-27]]  ([Location 944](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=944))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- There is even significant evidence that expressing our thoughts in writing can lead to benefits for our health and well-being.11 One of the most cited psychology papers of the 1990s found that “translating emotional events into words leads to profound social, psychological, and neural changes.” [[2023-12-14]]  ([Location 1006](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1006))
- In a wide range of controlled studies, writing about one’s inner experiences led to a drop in visits to the doctor, improved immune systems, and reductions in distress. Students who wrote about emotional topics showed improvements in their grades, professionals who had been laid off found new jobs more quickly, and staff members were absent from work at lower rates. The most amazing thing about these findings is that they didn’t rely on input from others. No one had to read or respond to what these people wrote down—the benefits came just from the act of writing. [[2023-12-14]]  ([Location 1009](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1009))
- Perhaps the most immediate benefit of capturing content outside our heads is that we escape what I call the “reactivity loop”—the hamster wheel of urgency, outrage, and sensationalism that characterizes so much of the Internet. The moment you first encounter an idea is the worst time to decide what it means. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1013](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1013))
- Twyla Tharp’s box reveals the true value of a simple container: it is easy to use, easy to understand, easy to create, and easy to maintain. It can be moved from place to place without losing its contents. A container requires no effort to identify, to share with others, and to put in storage when it’s no longer needed. We don’t need complex, sophisticated systems to be able to produce complex, sophisticated works. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1107](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1107))
- There’s a name for this phenomenon: the Cathedral Effect. [[2023-10-24]]  ([Location 1110](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1110))
- We know that the details of lighting, temperature, and the layout of a space dramatically affect how we feel and think. [[2023-10-24]]  ([Location 1112](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1112))
- There’s a name for this phenomenon: the Cathedral Effect.2 [[2025-11-25]]  ([Location 1113](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1113))
- The project becomes the main unit of organization for your digital files. Instead of having to sort your notes according to a complex hierarchy of topics and subtopics, you have to answer only one simple question: “In which project will this be most useful?” It assumes only that you are currently working on a certain set of projects, and that your information should be organized to support them. [[2023-10-24]]  ([Location 1149](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1149))
- This project-centric approach is increasingly finding its way into all knowledge work, a trend named the “Hollywood model” after the way films are made. As an article in the New York Times3 explains, “A project is identified; a team is assembled; it works together for precisely as long as is needed to complete the task; then the team disbands… The Hollywood model is now used to build bridges, design apps or start restaurants.” It is becoming more and more common for all of us to work across teams, departments, and even different companies to execute collaborative projects, and then once it’s over, each go our own way. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1194](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1194))
- That’s it—just three levels of hierarchy to encompass the thousands of notes I’ve accumulated over the years: the top-level PARA categories, the project folder, and the notes themselves. [[2023-10-29]]  ([Location 1270](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1270))
- Separating the capturing and organizing of ideas helps you stay present, notice what resonates, and leave the decision of what to do with them to a separate time (such as a “weekly review,” which I will cover in Chapter 9). [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1295](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1295))
- Most notes apps have an “inbox” or “daily notes” section where new notes you’ve captured are saved until you can revisit them and decide where they belong. Think of it as a waiting area where new ideas live until you are ready to digest them into your Second Brain. Separating the capturing and organizing of ideas helps you stay present, notice what resonates, and leave the decision of what to do with them to a separate time (such as a “weekly review,” which I will cover in Chapter 9). [[2023-10-25]]  ([Location 1297](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1297))
- we have to minimize the time we spend filing, labeling, tagging, and maintaining our digital notes. We can’t run the risk of all that effort going to waste. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1339](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1339))
- doesn’t matter how organized, aesthetically pleasing, or impressive your notetaking system is. It is only the steady completion of tangible wins that can infuse you with a sense of determination, momentum, and accomplishment. [[2023-10-25]]  ([Location 1387](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1387))
- Your job as a notetaker is to preserve the notes you’re taking on the things you discover in such a way that they can survive the journey into the future. [[2023-10-25]]  ([Location 1504](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1504))
- Discoverability is the element most often missing from people’s notes. It’s easy to save tons and tons of content, but turning it into a form that will be accessible in the future is another matter. [[2023-10-25]]  ([Location 1513](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1513))
- Progressive Summarization helps you focus on the content and the presentation of your notes,IV instead of spending too much time on labeling, tagging, linking, or other advanced features offered by many information management tools. It gives you a practical, easy thing to do that adds value even when you don’t have the energy for more challenging tasks. Most importantly, it keeps your attention on the substance of what you’re reading or learning, which is what matters in the long term. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1600](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1600))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- By saving the best excerpts from Wikipedia articles you read, you can create your own private encyclopedia with only the parts that are most relevant to you. [[2023-10-25]]  ([Location 1612](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1612))
- Much of the time we consume information without a specific purpose in mind. We might peruse the newspaper over breakfast, listen to a podcast while working out, or check out a newsletter to casually learn about a topic. We consume information to stay up to date, pass the time, entertain ourselves, and keep our minds engaged. These moments are some of the most valuable opportunities to capture tidbits of insight that you probably might not find otherwise. Because this casual reading and listening tends to range over a wide number of topics and interests, you are exposed to more diverse ideas than usual. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1621](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1621))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- I was driving and unable to write anything down, but as soon as we arrived I sat in the car for a few minutes and captured the ideas I remembered. This is actually a great way to filter down the volume of notes you’re taking—the best stuff always sticks in your mind for an hour or two. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1640](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1640))
- Progressive Summarization is not a method for remembering as much as possible—it is a method for forgetting as much as possible. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1691](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1691))
- Just as you listened for a feeling of internal resonance in deciding what content to save in the first place, the same rule applies for the insights within the note. Certain passages will move you, pique your attention, make your heart beat faster, or provoke you. Those are clear signals that you’ve found something important, and it’s time to add a highlight. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1730](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1730))
- When the opportunity arrives to do our best work, it’s not the time to start reading books and doing research. You need that research to already be done. [[2022-06-30]]  ([Location 1746](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1746))
- IV. As humans we are exquisitely sensitive to the way information is presented. In web design, a slight change of color for a button or a slightly reworded headline can easily have a double-digit impact on the number of visitors who click on it. Imagine if we put as much thought into how the information on our own devices is presented to us as we do into the public web. Even something as simple as an informative heading, a paragraph break, or a highlighted phrase can make it dramatically easier to absorb a piece of text. [[2023-10-25]]  ([Location 1772](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=1772))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- As humans we are exquisitely sensitive to the way information is presented. In web design, a slight change of color for a button or a slightly reworded headline can easily have a double-digit impact on the number of visitors who click on it. Imagine if we put as much thought into how the information on our own devices is presented to us as we do into the public web. Even something as simple as an informative heading, a paragraph break, or a highlighted phrase can make it dramatically easier to absorb a piece of text. - How do you create a Hemingway Bridge? Instead of burning through every last ounce of energy at the end of a work session, reserve the last few minutes to write down some of the following kinds of things in your digital notes: Write down ideas for next steps: At the end of a work session, write down what you think the next steps could be for the next one. Write down the current status: This could include your current biggest challenge, most important open question, or future roadblocks you expect. Write down any details you have in mind that are likely to be forgotten once you step away: Such as details about the characters in your story, the pitfalls of the event you’re planning, or the subtle considerations of the product you’re designing. - The process of knowing yourself can seem mystical, but I see it as eminently practical. It starts with noticing what resonates with you. Noticing what seems to call out to you in the external world and gives you a sense of déjà vu. There is a universe of thoughts and ideas and emotions within you. Over time, you can uncover new layers of yourself and new facets of your identity. You search outside yourself to search within yourself, knowing that everything you find has always been a part of you. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2949](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2949))
- I discovered something through that experience: that self-expression is a fundamental human need. Self-expression is as vital to our survival as food or shelter. We must be able to share the stories of our lives—from the small moments of what happened today at school to our grandest theories of what life is about. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2971](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2971))
- I’m here to tell you that there is no reason to wait. The world is desperate to hear what you know. You can change lives by sharing yourself with others. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2976](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2976))
- Some of what you say might not resonate with others or provide value to them, but occasionally, you will strike on something—a way of seeing, a perspective, a story—that blows people’s minds and visibly transforms how they see the world. It could be someone you’re having coffee with, a client or customer, or your online followers. In those moments, the vast chasm that separates us as humans is bridged. For a brief moment, you get to feel in your bones that we are all in this together. We are all part of a vast tattered fabric of humanity, and your highest calling is simply to play your part in it. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2981](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2981))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- This isn’t a “take it or leave it” ideology where you must accept all of it or none of it. If any part doesn’t make sense or doesn’t resonate with you, put it aside. Mix and match the tools and techniques you’ve learned in this book to suit your needs. This is how you ensure your Second Brain remains a lifelong companion through the seasons of your life. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2992](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2992))
- Keep what resonates (Capture) Save for actionability (Organize) Find the essence (Distill) Show your work (Express) [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2997](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2997))
- If at any point you feel overwhelmed, take a step back and focus on what is immediately necessary: your most important projects and priorities. Scale back to only the notes you need to move those priorities forward. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 2999](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=2999))
    - Tags: [[favorite]] 
- Are you hoping to remember more? Focus on developing the practice of capturing and organizing your notes according to your projects, commitments, and interests using PARA. Are you hoping to connect ideas and develop your ability to plan, influence, and grow in your personal and professional life? Experiment with consistently distilling and refining your notes using Progressive Summarization and revisiting them during weekly reviews. Are you committed to producing more and better output with less frustration and stress? Focus on creating one Intermediate Packet at a time and looking for opportunities to share them in ever more bold ways. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 3003](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=3003))
- Choose a capture tool. I recommend starting with a read later app to begin saving any article or other piece of online content you’re interested in for later consumption. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 3015](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=3015))
- Get inspired by identifying your twelve favorite problems. Make a list of some of your favorite problems, save the list as a note, and revisit it any time you need ideas for what to capture. Use these open-ended questions as a filter to decide which content is worth keeping. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 3019](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=3019))
- Make progress on one deliverable. Choose a project deliverable you’re responsible for and, using the Express techniques of Archipelago of Ideas, Hemingway Bridge, and Dial Down the Scope, see if you can make decisive progress on it using only the notes in your Second Brain. [[2022-07-07]]  ([Location 3029](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B09LVVN9L3&location=3029))

notes.title: Determined
notes.body:
## Highlights
- Turtles All the Way Down [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 76](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=76))
- So, it seems that William James was giving a lecture about the nature of life and the universe. Afterward, an old woman came up and said, “Professor James, you have it all wrong.” To which James asked, “How so, madam?” “Things aren’t at all like you said,” she replied. “The world is on the back of a gigantic turtle.” “Hmm.” said James, bemused. “That may be so, but where does that turtle stand?” “On the back of another turtle,” she answered. “But madam,” said James indulgently, “where does that turtle stand?” To which the old woman responded triumphantly: “It’s no use, Professor James. It’s turtles all the way down!”[*] [[2023-12-15]]  ([Location 79](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=79))
- The Sound of Music, “Nothing comes from nothing, nothing ever could.”[*] [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 101](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=101))
- And that it makes as little sense to hate someone as to hate a tornado because it supposedly decided to level your house, or to love a lilac because it supposedly decided to make a wonderful fragrance. That’s what it means to conclude that there is no free will. [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 148](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=148))
- put all the scientific results together, from all the relevant scientific disciplines, and there’s no room for free will.[*] [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 194](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=194))
    - Note: Not sure this is true. If physics underlies it all and the universe by the best descriptions we know to date is a computation, then isn’t that sufficient?
- The book’s intentionally ambiguous title reflects these two halves—it is both about the science of why there is no free will and the science of how we might best live once we accept that. [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 215](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=215))
- the book will most often be taking on these “compatibilists.” [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 228](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=228))
- my goal isn’t to convince you that there’s no free will; it will suffice if you merely conclude that there’s so much less free will than you thought that you have to change your thinking about some truly important things. [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 250](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=250))
- I’ve concluded that they usually boil down to three sentences: Wow, there’ve been all these cool advances in neuroscience, all reinforcing the conclusion that ours is a deterministic world. Some of those neuroscience findings challenge our notions of agency, moral responsibility, and deservedness so deeply that one must conclude that there is no free will. Nah, it still exists. [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 258](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=258))
- People define free will differently. Many focus on agency, whether a person can control their actions, act with intent. Other definitions concern whether, when a behavior occurs, the person knows that there are alternatives available. Others are less concerned with what you do than with vetoing what you don’t want to do. [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 281](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=281))
- It’s virtually required to start this topic with the dead White male Pierre Simon Laplace, the eighteenth-/nineteenth-century French polymath (it’s also required that you call him a polymath, as he contributed to mathematics, physics, engineering, astronomy, and philosophy). [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 299](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=299))
- Ask someone to name their favorite detergent, and if you have unconsciously cued them earlier with the word ocean, they become more likely to answer, “Tide.” As an important measure of where meta-level consciousness comes in, suppose the person realizes what the researcher is up to and, wanting to show that they can’t be manipulated, decides that they won’t say “Tide,” even if it is their favorite. Their freedom has been just as constrained, [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 311](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=311))
- we congratulate the graduate on all she’s accomplished and move out of the way of the garbage guy without glancing at him. [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 334](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=334))
- fact, a huge percentage of the research done concerning the free-will debate revolves around intent, often microscopically examining the role of intent in the seconds before a behavior happens. Entire conferences, edited volumes, careers, have been spent on those few seconds, and in many ways, this focus is at the heart of arguments supporting compatibilism; [[2023-12-20]]  ([Location 351](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=351))
- Psychology studies also show how the sense of agency can be illusory. In one study, pushing a button would be followed immediately by a light going on . . . some of the time. The percentage of time the light would go on was varied; subjects were then asked how much control they felt they had over the light. People consistently overestimate how reliably the light occurs, feeling that they control it.[*] [[2023-12-21]]  ([Location 456](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=456))
- What the science in this book ultimately teaches is that there is no meaning. There’s no answer to “Why?” beyond “This happened because of what came just before, which happened because of what came just before that.” There is nothing but an empty, indifferent universe in which, occasionally, atoms come together temporarily to form things we each call Me. A whole field [[2023-12-31]]  ([Location 5881](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=5881))
- Stephen Cave titled a much-discussed June 2016 article in The Atlantic “There’s No Such Thing as Free Will . . . but We’re Better Off Believing in It Anyway.”[*] He just might be right. Chapter 2 discussed a study in which a sense of “illusory will” could be induced in people. One subgroup of subjects, however, was resistant to this—individuals with clinical depression. Depression is often framed as a sufferer having a cognitively distorted sense of “learned helplessness,” where the reality of some loss in the past becomes mistakenly perceived as an inevitable future. In this study, though, it was not that depressed individuals were cognitively distorted, underestimating their actual control. Instead they were accurate compared with everyone else’s overestimates. [[2023-12-31]]  ([Location 5898](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B0BSKQ5ZDM&location=5898))

notes.ref: Free Will
notes.title: Free Will
notes.body:
## Highlights
- Therefore, while it is true to say that a person would have done otherwise if he had chosen to do otherwise, this does not deliver the kind of free will that most people seem to cherish—because a person’s “choices” merely appear in his mind as though sprung from the void. [[2024-05-10]]  ([Location 307](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B006IDG2T6&location=307))
- From the perspective of your conscious awareness, you are no more responsible for the next thing you think (and therefore do) than you are for the fact that you were born into this world. [[2024-05-10]]  ([Location 309](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B006IDG2T6&location=309))
- Losing a belief in free will has not made me fatalistic—in fact, it has increased my feelings of freedom. My hopes, fears, and neuroses seem less personal and indelible. There is no telling how much I might change in the future. Just as one wouldn’t draw a lasting conclusion about oneself on the basis of a brief experience of indigestion, one needn’t do so on the basis of how one has thought or behaved for vast stretches of time in the past. A creative change of inputs to the system—learning new skills, forming new relationships, adopting new habits of attention—may radically transform one’s life. [[2024-04-06]]  ([Location 407](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B006IDG2T6&location=407))
- Becoming sensitive to the background causes of one’s thoughts and feelings can—paradoxically—allow for greater creative control over one’s life. [[2024-04-06]]  ([Location 412](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B006IDG2T6&location=412))
- It is one thing to bicker with your wife because you are in a bad mood; it is another to realize that your mood and behavior have been caused by low blood sugar. This understanding reveals you to be a biochemical puppet, of course, but it also allows you to grab hold of one of your strings: A bite of food may be all that your personality requires. Getting behind our conscious thoughts and feelings can allow us to steer a more intelligent course through our lives (while knowing, of course, that we are ultimately being steered). [[2024-04-06]]  ([Location 413](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B006IDG2T6&location=413))

notes.ref: Readwise
notes.title: Readwise
notes.body:
# Readwise

Highlights synced from Readwise — books, articles, podcasts. Entries
keep their original frontmatter (`title`, `author`, `image`) so the
card grid renders covers straight from Amazon image URLs.

```base
filters:
  and:
    - file.folder.contains("Full Document Contents")
    - or:
        - type.contains("article")
        - type.contains("tweet")
        - type.contains("podcast")
        - type.contains("book")
views:
  - type: cards
    name: Recent
    sort:
      - property: published
        direction: DESC
```

notes.ref: Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting
notes.title: Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting
notes.body: # Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting

frontmatter.author: Serge Wroclawski
frontmatter.title: Implementing Profit First With Plain Text Accounting
frontmatter.summary: Plain Text Accounting is a method of bookkeeping that stores account and transaction information in plain text files. Profit First is an accounting method that prioritizes core business expenses and recommends using different bank accounts to allocate income. Integrating Plain Text Accounting with Profit First is straightforward if you have multiple bank accounts, but if you have a single account, it becomes more challenging. One approach is to use "virtual postings" in Plain Text Accounting software to create virtual accounts that mirror the Profit First accounts. However, this method requires recording transactions twice and can be more difficult to verify and balance. Another option is to find a bank that supports multiple accounts and use the Profit First model directly.

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: What Alcohol Does to Your Body, Brain & Health | Huberman Lab Podcast #86
frontmatter.summary: In this episode, I discuss the physiological effects that drinking alcohol has on the brain and body at different levels of consumption and over time. I also describe genetic differences that predispose certain individuals to alcoholism, binge and habit-drinking. I explain alcohol metabolism in simple terms and how it effectively acts as a poison, leading to cellular stress and damage. I then explain that it impacts neuronal function and changes our thinking and behavior – hallmarks of inebriation. I also discuss how alcohol consumption of different amounts impacts inflammation, stress, neurodegeneration, and cancer risk and negatively impacts the gut microbiome, brain thickness, hormone balance, mood and feelings of motivation. Additionally, I discuss the biology of hangovers and describe science-based strategies to mitigate the severity of a hangover. Since alcohol is one of the most widely consumed recreational substances, this episode ought to be of relevance to everyone.  Indeed, even low-to-moderate alcoho...

notes.ref: The Science of Creativity & How to Enhance Creative Innovation Huberman Lab Podcast 103
notes.title: The Science of Creativity & How to Enhance Creative Innovation | Huberman Lab Podcast 103
notes.body: # The Science of Creativity & How to Enhance Creative Innovation | Huberman Lab Podcast 103

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: The Science of Creativity & How to Enhance Creative Innovation | Huberman Lab Podcast 103
frontmatter.summary: In this podcast episode, the host discusses the science of creativity and how to enhance creative innovation. The neural circuits responsible for creativity have been defined, and the processes within the brain and body that lead to creativity are well known. Creativity stems from a small subset of neural structures that need to be activated in a specific sequence. The creative process involves both divergent thinking, which is about exploration and generating many ideas, and convergent thinking, which is about focus and persistence to find the correct answer. Dopamine plays a crucial role in both types of thinking, and there are ways to enhance dopamine levels to improve creative thinking. Open monitoring meditation has been shown to enhance divergent thinking, while focus and persistence are key for convergent thinking. Elevating dopamine levels is beneficial for creativity, but too much dopamine can hinder divergent thinking. The podcast also discusses a behavioral tool that can selectively elevate dopamine i...

They handle tasks like data processing, proofreading, and voice assistance well, though they aren't as powerful as big cloud-based models. For many people, local LLMs provide a reliable, private, and cost-effective solution that works even without internet.

notes.ref: There’s an Invisible Workload That Drags Men Down, Too
notes.title: There’s an Invisible Workload That Drags Men Down, Too
notes.body: # There’s an Invisible Workload That Drags Men Down, Too

frontmatter.author: Josh Levs
frontmatter.title: There’s an Invisible Workload That Drags Men Down, Too
frontmatter.summary: Many men are suffering from anxiety, depression, and high levels of stress, but their mental health struggles often go unnoticed because they are less likely to openly express their feelings or seek help. An article on Money.com argued that women bear the "invisible workload" of family life, but it failed to acknowledge that men also have their own invisible workload and face challenges in balancing work and family. Men are often the primary breadwinners and shoulder financial burdens, which can contribute to their stress and anxiety. It is important for couples to have open discussions about their worries and support each other, and for society to challenge outdated perceptions of gender roles and provide more support for fathers.

notes.ref: The Science of Making & Breaking Habits Huberman Lab Podcast #53
notes.title: The Science of Making & Breaking Habits | Huberman Lab Podcast #53
notes.body: # The Science of Making & Breaking Habits | Huberman Lab Podcast #53

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: The Science of Making & Breaking Habits | Huberman Lab Podcast #53
frontmatter.summary: Habits are reflexive actions that our nervous system learns, and they can either serve us well or undermine our goals. There is a whole field of neuroscience and psychology dedicated to understanding habit formation and breaking. The formation of habits involves biology, such as cells, hormones, and neural pathways. Habits can be strengthened by being specific about when and where they are performed, and by associating reward mechanisms with the entire time frame of the habit. By understanding the neuroscience and psychology of habits, we can better form and break them.

notes.ref: A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface
notes.title: A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface
notes.body: # A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface

frontmatter.author: Wendy Wood and David T. Neal
frontmatter.title: A New Look at Habits and the Habit–Goal Interface
frontmatter.summary: This article explores the mechanisms underlying habitual control of responding and the ways in which habits interface with goals. It suggests that goals can direct habits by motivating repetition and promoting exposure to cues that trigger habits. Habits, once acquired, can be performed without the mediation of a goal. The article also discusses the role of context cues in activating habit responses and the variability in habit performance depending on the availability or accessibility of explicit goals. Overall, the article highlights the complex relationship between habits and goals in shaping human behavior.

notes.ref: 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail
notes.title: 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail
notes.body: # 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail

frontmatter.author: James Clear
frontmatter.title: 5 Common Mistakes That Cause New Habits to Fail
frontmatter.summary: Around 81 to 92 percent of New Year's resolutions fail, indicating that it is challenging to stick to new habits. The article highlights five common mistakes that cause new habits to fail and provides solutions for each problem. These mistakes include trying to change everything at once, starting with a habit that is too big, seeking a result rather than focusing on the behavior, not changing the environment, and assuming that small changes don't add up. The article emphasizes the importance of focusing on one habit at a time, starting with small and manageable habits, and creating an environment that promotes good habits.

Her primary research focuses on habits and behavior change, as well as the psychology of gender. Wood is the author of the book "Good Habits, Bad Habits" and has received numerous awards for her research. She has also made influential contributions in the study of sex and gender, as well as attitudes and social influence. Wood's research has been widely recognized and cited in the field of psychology.

notes.title: A Science-Supported Journaling Protocol to Improve Mental & Physical Health
notes.body: # A Science-Supported Journaling Protocol to Improve Mental & Physical Health

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: A Science-Supported Journaling Protocol to Improve Mental & Physical Health
frontmatter.summary: This document discusses a science-supported journaling protocol that has been shown to improve mental and physical health. The protocol, researched by Dr. James Pennebaker, involves writing about a difficult or traumatic experience for 15 to 30 minutes without stopping or paying attention to grammar or spelling. The writing can be done by hand or typed. Over 200 peer-reviewed studies have demonstrated the positive effects of this journaling method, including improved memory, decision-making, sleep, immunity, and symptoms of autoimmune disorders. The protocol can be implemented in a relatively short amount of time and has lasting benefits for both body and mind.

notes.ref: Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX
notes.title: Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX
notes.body: # Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX

frontmatter.author: Raluca Budiu
frontmatter.title: Fitts's Law and Its Applications in UX
frontmatter.summary: Fitts's Law is a principle that states the movement time to a target depends on the size of the target and the distance to the target. It was developed by psychologist Paul Fitts, who studied the relationship between human movement and design. Fitts's Law has implications for user experience (UX) design, particularly in terms of optimizing target size and distance to target. The law suggests that larger targets and closer targets are easier and faster to acquire. Designers can apply Fitts's Law by making targets bigger, using icons with labels, avoiding crowding targets, and placing related targets close to each other.

notes.ref: Chunking Learning Technique for Better Memory
notes.title: Chunking: Learning Technique for Better Memory
notes.body: # Chunking: Learning Technique for Better Memory

frontmatter.author: Sprouts
frontmatter.title: Chunking: Learning Technique for Better Memory
frontmatter.summary: Chunking is a learning technique that helps improve memory by breaking large bits of information into smaller pieces or chunks. By doing this, we can understand and remember the information more easily. Our short-term memory can only hold about 4 chunks of information at once, so chunking allows us to make space for new information or move chunks into long-term memory for storage and later recall. There are different ways to chunk, such as breaking a larger piece into smaller bits or identifying patterns. Adding context to the chunks helps make the transfer into long-term memory more effective. Chunking can be applied to various learning scenarios, from studying complex topics to learning a musical piece, and it can also be used to make decisions more efficiently.

notes.ref: Individual Differences in Working Memory and Reading
notes.title: Individual Differences in Working Memory and Reading
notes.body: # Individual Differences in Working Memory and Reading

frontmatter.title: Individual Differences in Working Memory and Reading
frontmatter.summary: The document discusses the relationship between working memory capacity and reading comprehension. The study involved administering different tests to measure working memory span, reading comprehension, and word span. The results showed that the reading span test, which measured the ability to recall sentence final words while maintaining perfect recall, correlated well with reading comprehension performance. In contrast, the traditional word span test did not show significant correlations with comprehension measures. The study also examined the relationship between working memory span and specific components of comprehension, such as fact retrieval and pronominal reference, and found significant correlations. Overall, the findings suggest that working memory capacity plays a role in reading comprehension.

frontmatter.title: Feynman on memory and triangulation
frontmatter.summary: In this document, physicist Richard Feynman provides tips on studying physics. He emphasizes the importance of understanding the interrelationships of nature rather than simply memorizing formulas. Feynman encourages students to "triangulate" from what is known to what is unknown, using logic and known facts to fill in gaps in knowledge. He explains that as long as one knows enough and doesn't forget too much, it is possible to continually recreate forgotten information. Feynman concludes that the real utility of physicists lies in discovering new laws and making new discoveries by triangulating from the known to the previously unknown.

notes.ref: Psychology of Habit
notes.title: Psychology of Habit
notes.body: # Psychology of Habit

frontmatter.author: Annual Reviews
frontmatter.title: Psychology of Habit
frontmatter.summary: Habits are characterized by their cognitive, motivational, and neurobiological properties. They form as people repeat actions in a given context while pursuing goals and become the default mode of response. Habits are influenced by associative and reward-learning mechanisms and are activated by recurring context cues. Computational models explain the cognitive processes involved in habit learning and performance, highlighting the interaction between habits and deliberate goal pursuit. The neurobiology of habits involves the sensorimotor cortico-basal ganglia loop, and multiple brain regions participate in both habitual and goal-directed control. Factors such as stress and learning mechanisms can shift the balance between habits and deliberate goal pursuit. People often infer underlying motives from the frequency of habit performance, even though habits are not necessarily goal-driven.

notes.ref: Double-Entry Accounting What It Is and Why It Matters
notes.title: Double-Entry Accounting: What It Is and Why It Matters
notes.body: # Double-Entry Accounting: What It Is and Why It Matters

frontmatter.author: Hillary Crawford
frontmatter.title: Double-Entry Accounting: What It Is and Why It Matters
frontmatter.summary: Double-entry accounting is a bookkeeping system that requires two entries - one debit and one credit - for every transaction. Unlike single-entry accounting, which focuses on tracking revenue and expenses, double-entry accounting also tracks assets, liabilities, and equity. By recording debits and credits, this system provides a more complete picture of how money is moving through a business. Double-entry accounting is commonly used with accounting software, which automates much of the process. It is important for businesses with significant assets, liabilities, or inventory to use double-entry accounting for a more accurate understanding of their financial health.

notes.ref: Do We Have Free Will Robert Sapolsky & Andrew Huberman
notes.title: Do We Have Free Will? | Robert Sapolsky & Andrew Huberman
notes.body: # Do We Have Free Will? | Robert Sapolsky & Andrew Huberman

frontmatter.author: Huberman Lab Clips
frontmatter.title: Do We Have Free Will? | Robert Sapolsky & Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.summary: According to Robert Sapolsky and Andrew Huberman, there is a debate about free will and the ability to make choices. Sapolsky argues that there is no free will because our behavior and choices are influenced by numerous factors, such as our sensory environment, hormone levels, and upbringing. He believes that all these factors are ultimately one, making it impossible to separate freewill from these influences. Huberman asks if recognizing these influences can give us a small sense of free will, but Sapolsky disagrees, stating that while change is possible, it is not through volitional control. Instead, change happens through external circumstances and understanding the potential for change.

notes.ref: Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form
notes.title: Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form
notes.body: # Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form

frontmatter.author: Anne Trafton
frontmatter.title: Distinctive brain pattern helps habits form
frontmatter.summary: MIT neuroscientists have discovered that certain neurons in the brain are responsible for marking the beginning and end of routine behaviors, a process known as "chunking." These neurons fire at the start of a learned routine, go quiet during the routine, and then fire again once the routine is complete. This task-bracketing is important for initiating and completing routines. The researchers trained rats to perform a particular sequence of lever presses and observed the same firing patterns in the rats' brains as they had seen in previous habit studies. The study provides evidence that the firing pattern corresponds specifically to the initiation and termination of a learned routine.

notes.ref: Ann Graybiel
notes.title: Ann Graybiel
notes.body: # Ann Graybiel

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Ann Graybiel
frontmatter.summary: Ann Martin Graybiel is a renowned neuroscientist and an expert on the basal ganglia. Her research has focused on the physiology of the striatum and its role in habit formation, implicit learning, and various neurological disorders. Graybiel's discoveries have shed light on the neural mechanisms underlying Parkinson's disease, Huntington's disease, and obsessive-compulsive disorder. She has received numerous awards and recognition for her contributions to neuroscience, including the National Medal of Science and the Kavli Prize. Graybiel is a member of prestigious scientific organizations and continues to make significant contributions to the field of neuroscience.

notes.ref: Fitts's Law
notes.title: Fitts's Law
notes.body: # Fitts's Law

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Fitts's Law
frontmatter.summary: Fitts's law is a predictive model used in human-computer interaction and ergonomics to determine the time required to move to a target area. It states that the time is based on the ratio of the distance to the target and the width of the target. The law is used to model pointing tasks, both physical and virtual. There have been various formulations and extensions of the law, including adjustments for accuracy and incorporating information theory. Fitts's law has provided insights into user interface design principles and can be used as a performance metric for interfaces.

notes.ref: How to Enhance Performance & Learning by Applying a Growth Mindset Huberman Lab Podcast
notes.title: How to Enhance Performance & Learning by Applying a Growth Mindset | Huberman Lab Podcast
notes.body: # How to Enhance Performance & Learning by Applying a Growth Mindset | Huberman Lab Podcast

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: How to Enhance Performance & Learning by Applying a Growth Mindset | Huberman Lab Podcast
frontmatter.summary: In this episode of the Huberman Lab Podcast, Dr. Andrew Huberman discusses the concept of growth mindset and how it can enhance performance and learning. Growth mindset is the idea that our abilities are not fixed, but can be developed through effort and practice. It involves distancing our identity from our performance and attaching it to the process of learning and getting better. Dr. Huberman explores the research on growth mindset and its application in various domains, such as education and sports. He also highlights the work of Carol Dweck, who pioneered the field of growth mindset. Additionally, he introduces the concept of stress-enhancing mindset and how combining it with growth mindset can further improve performance. Throughout the episode, Dr. Huberman provides practical tools and tips for implementing growth mindset in everyday life.

notes.ref: The Magical Mystery Four How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why
notes.title: The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?
notes.body: # The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?

frontmatter.author: Nelson Cowan
frontmatter.title: The Magical Mystery Four: How Is Working Memory Capacity Limited, and Why?
frontmatter.summary: Working memory capacity is limited to holding about 3 to 5 meaningful items at once. This capacity limit is important for tasks that rely on working memory, such as language comprehension, problem solving, and planning. The limit can be observed by preventing processing strategies like rehearsal and grouping. Working memory capacity varies among individuals and can predict differences in intellectual ability. There are ongoing debates about the reasons for this capacity limit, with hypotheses suggesting both weaknesses and strengths in having a limited working memory capacity.

notes.ref: Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking
notes.title: Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking
notes.body: # Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking

frontmatter.author: Elizabeth Whitworth
frontmatter.title: Learn and Remember More With Information Chunking
frontmatter.summary: Information chunking is a process in which memories are consolidated into "chunks" of related information in the brain. It is a vital part of the learning process as it helps organize information and improve memory. Working memory and long-term memory are both involved in information chunking. Working memory holds the information actively being processed, while long-term memory stores information for future reference. Choking can occur when working memory cannot hold all the information needed to solve a problem, but chunking helps reduce the risk of choking by condensing information.

notes.title: Psychology asserts that children of the 1960s and 1970s did not become emotionally strong thanks to better parenting, but because they grew up with enough daily neglect to learn to self-regulate, solve problems on their own, and develop a resilience that modern comforts make difficult to build
notes.body: # Psychology asserts that children of the 1960s and 1970s did not become emotionally strong thanks to better parenting, but because they grew up with enough daily neglect to learn to self-regulate, solve problems on their own, and develop a resilience that modern comforts make difficult to build

frontmatter.author: ECOticias.com
frontmatter.title: Psychology asserts that children of the 1960s and 1970s did not become emotionally strong thanks to better parenting, but because they grew up with enough daily neglect to learn to self-regulate, solve problems on their own, and develop a resilience that modern comforts make difficult to build
frontmatter.summary: Children in the 1960s and 1970s became emotionally strong partly because they had more freedom to solve problems and manage feelings on their own. Today, overprotective parenting can increase anxiety and depression in young people by limiting their chances to learn self-regulation. Giving kids safe chances to make choices and face small challenges helps build resilience without neglecting their safety.

notes.ref: What Is Punctuation For
notes.title: What Is Punctuation For?
notes.body: # What Is Punctuation For?

frontmatter.author: Livia Gershon
frontmatter.title: What Is Punctuation For?
frontmatter.summary: Punctuation has evolved over centuries from its role in guiding oral reading in ancient times to becoming a tool for indicating pauses and conveying meaning in written text. In classical Greece and Rome, terms like "comma," "colon," and "period" referred to units of language rather than marks on paper. As writing shifted towards being read silently, punctuation began to serve both as a guide to speaking patterns and logic, eventually transitioning to emphasizing the construction of sentences on the page. The emergence of the semicolon in the late 15th century marked a shift towards a more rational and logical understanding of punctuation.

notes.ref: Internal Family Systems Model
notes.title: Internal Family Systems Model
notes.body: # Internal Family Systems Model

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Internal Family Systems Model
frontmatter.summary: The Internal Family Systems Model (IFS) is a psychotherapy approach developed by Richard C. Schwartz in the 1980s. It uses family systems theory to understand how different parts or subpersonalities within an individual are organized. Each part has its own perspective, interests, and memories. The goal of IFS therapy is to heal wounded parts and establish a cooperative relationship between the Self and each part. Therapists help clients access their Self and build trust with their Protector parts. This model has been widely used and has training institutes worldwide.

notes.ref: Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing
notes.title: Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing
notes.body: # Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing

frontmatter.author: Reuters Fact Check
frontmatter.title: Edmund Burke Did Not Say Evil Triumphs When Good Men Do Nothing
frontmatter.summary: The popular phrase "The only thing necessary for the triumph of evil is for good men to do nothing" has been misattributed to Edmund Burke. There is no evidence that Burke actually said these words, and the exact quote has not been found in his writings. The misattribution of the quote has persisted, with notable figures such as President John F. Kennedy using it in speeches. Burke did make a similar statement in his writings, but it is not an exact match to the misattributed quote. The origin of the misattribution is likely a paraphrase based on Burke's work.

notes.ref: Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention
notes.title: Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention
notes.body: # Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention

frontmatter.author: Andrew Huberman
frontmatter.title: Tools to Enhance Working Memory & Attention
frontmatter.summary: Working memory is a type of memory that allows us to hold small amounts of information in our mind for short periods of time. It is closely related to attention and can be improved through various tools and techniques. Working memory is different from long-term memory, which involves the storage of facts and procedures, and short-term memory, which acts as a temporary memory bank. The formation of short and long-term memories involves neuroplasticity, the brain's ability to change and adapt in response to experience. Different types of neuroplasticity, such as long-term potentiation and long-term depression, play a role in memory formation.

frontmatter.title: Sam Altman's basic-income study is out. Here's what it found.
frontmatter.summary: Sam Altman's basic-income study gave low-income participants $1,000 a month for three years. Recipients spent most of the money on food and rent, and experienced less financial stress, though the benefits faded over time. The study showed that while cash payments helped with immediate needs, they did not significantly improve overall health or long-term financial stability.

frontmatter.author: Sarah
frontmatter.title: 4 Brilliant Books for Improving Your Daily Life (Via HABITS)
frontmatter.summary: This article recommends four books for improving daily life through the power of habits. The first book, "Mini Habits," teaches readers to break tasks into small, manageable steps to overcome procrastination. The second book, "Tiny Habits," delves into the reasons behind habits and provides strategies for building and undoing them. The third book, "Elastic Habits," expands on the concept of mini habits and offers methods for designing habits that can be adjusted based on goals. The fourth book, "Atomic Habits," is praised for its clear and insightful approach to building better habits using four simple steps.

notes.ref: Working Memory
notes.title: Working Memory
notes.body: # Working Memory

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Working Memory
frontmatter.summary: Working memory is a complex cognitive process that involves the temporary storage and manipulation of information. It is essential for tasks such as problem-solving, decision-making, and learning. Research has shown that working memory is influenced by genetic factors and can vary between individuals. Additionally, working memory capacity tends to decline with age and can be affected by neurological conditions such as Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. Understanding the mechanisms and limitations of working memory is crucial for developing interventions and treatments for cognitive impairments.

notes.ref: Reading span task
notes.title: Reading span task
notes.body: # Reading span task

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Reading span task
frontmatter.summary: The reading span task (RST) is a verbal working memory test that requires participants to read series of unconnected sentences and remember the final word of each sentence. It was the first instance of the "complex span" tasks, which require both storage and processing elements of working memory. Variants of the RST have been developed, including a computerized version that allows for direct comparisons across different language groups. The RST has been found to be more strongly related to reading comprehension than simple verbal tests like word span.

notes.ref: Yerkes–Dodson law
notes.title: Yerkes–Dodson law
notes.body: # Yerkes–Dodson law

frontmatter.author: wikipedia.org
frontmatter.title: Yerkes–Dodson law
frontmatter.summary: The Yerkes-Dodson law, developed in 1908 by psychologists Robert M. Yerkes and John Dillingham Dodson, is an empirical relationship that states that performance increases with arousal, but only up to a certain point. The relationship between arousal and performance differs depending on the task, with simple tasks improving as arousal increases and complex tasks declining after a certain point. The law can be decomposed into two factors, with the energizing effect of arousal contributing to performance improvement and the negative effects of arousal impacting cognitive processes. Other models of arousal, such as the Flow theory, offer alternative perspectives. Additionally, there is evidence of a correlation between stress hormones and cognitive performance that resembles the Yerkes-Dodson curve.
